source.title: Gate on fast-tier p99 and standard-tier p95
source.author: Sahana Iyer
source.description: Summary: This PR adds tier-aware SLO gating to perf-canary for the Workload-aware Batching Defaults launch. We now fail the perf-canary run if: (1) FAST tier p99 latency regresses beyond threshold (tail-latency protection gate), or (2) STANDARD tier p95 latency regresses beyond threshold (steady-state gate). The intent is to stop merges that silently violate the published tier SLOs while still allowing legitimate improvements to land with a controlled baseline refresh process. Motivation: As we move batching config from ad-hoc per-route knobs to three customer-facing tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient), we need CI guardrails that match what we’ll ship. Previously, canary gating was based on a single aggregate p95 and a single scenario per model. That missed fast-tier tail regressions (p99) and could overreact to noise in standard tier when concurrency ramps changed. What changed (high level): (1) Adds a tier_slo_gates.yaml config describing which percentile to gate on per tier, thresholds, and the scenario → tier mapping. (2) Extends the results parser to ingest tier labels from benchmark-lab output (scenario metadata), and compute per-tier regression deltas against the stored baseline. (3) Adds a baseline refresh workflow with explicit approvals, including a short “baseline justification” note that is stored next to the refreshed baseline file. (4) Adds a regression triage runbook tailored for tiered batching: how to distinguish scheduler regressions vs workload-mix drift, and what artifacts to attach to the Linear issue. Details: Gating rules: FAST tier: gate on p99 end-to-end latency, primary metric = request_latency_ms (includes queue + prefill + decode), scenario group = mixed_workload_streaming_shortprefill + mixed_workload_nonstreaming_shortprefill. Threshold default = +6% relative regression OR +25ms absolute (whichever triggers first) with a minimum sample size requirement. STANDARD tier: gate on p95 end-to-end latency, scenario group = mixed_workload_balanced + summarization_mediumprefill. Threshold default = +5% relative regression OR +35ms absolute. Note: COST-EFFICIENT tier is intentionally not a merge-blocking gate in this PR; we still compute and report it, but it only posts a warning annotation. This matches the staged rollout plan and avoids blocking on known variance for aggressive batching experiments. Noise controls: (a) sample-size gate: if we have < N requests for a tier/scenario, the gate is “insufficient data” and does not fail the run (but posts a warning). (b) warm-up exclusion: ignore first K requests in each scenario to reduce cold-start variance. (c) optional “max allowed stddev” heuristic (disabled by default) included behind a flag for future tuning. Baseline refresh process (new): We now store baselines under baselines/<model_family>/<hardware_class>/baseline.json and include a matching baselines/<...>/baseline.meta.txt with: timestamp, perf-canary run URL, benchmark-lab revision, and a one-paragraph human summary of why the refresh is safe (e.g., “intentional latency improvement due to scheduler tweak”). To refresh: run ./scripts/refresh_baseline.sh --model-family llama --hw h100 --scenario-set tiers --run-id <canary-run> then open a PR containing only baseline files + meta. That PR must have approvals from one Runtime reviewer and one SRE reviewer. Regression triage notes (new docs): Added docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md. Key guidance: verify the tier label in the artifact, check queue depth & batch-size distribution in the attached benchmark-lab plots, compare streaming vs non-streaming deltas (fast-tier p99 is usually driven by admission/wait budget), and tag the resulting Linear issue with runtime+slo-gate. Also includes “when to refresh baseline” vs “when to block merge and fix” decision table. Changed areas/files (high level): - config/tier_slo_gates.yaml: new canonical gating config - src/gating/tier_slo_gate.py: new tier-aware gate implementation - src/results/parser.py: scenario metadata → tier mapping + percentile extraction - src/cli.py: new flags for tier gating, warm-up exclusion - scripts/refresh_baseline.sh: baseline refresh helper + guardrails - docs/baseline_refresh.md: refresh procedure + required approvals - docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md: triage playbook - .github/workflows/perf-canary.yml: upload tier gate summary, annotate PRs Commit summary: 1) parser: ingest scenario metadata and emit tier breakdown 2) gating: implement fast p99 + standard p95 gates w/ thresholds 3) scripts/docs: add baseline refresh + triage docs 4) workflow: upload artifacts + PR annotations Validation: - Ran on 6 recent canary runs for top model families; no false failures after warm-up exclusion and sample-size checks - Verified that a known fast-tier p99 regression (synthetic +12% added wait budget) fails the gate - Verified that a known standard-tier improvement (scheduler prefill/decode split change) passes and baseline can be refreshed cleanly Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Add an optional gate for COST-EFFICIENT tier once variance is reduced (likely after we stabilize queue backpressure heuristics) - Integrate “reason codes” (profile selection telemetry) into the canary report when available from runtime 1.22 - Expand coverage to additional regions/hardware classes once Dedicated fleet baselines exist
source.conversation: Review notes: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Can we ensure this won’t page people for noise? I’m worried about p99 being unstable on low-volume scenarios. Also, can we make sure insufficient-data doesn’t fail merges. Sahana Iyer (author): Yes — insufficient-data is a warning only; the gate only fails if we have >= min_samples. I also added warm-up exclusion and kept absolute+relative thresholds to reduce sensitivity. Rafael Mendes: Baseline refresh needs an explicit paper trail. Can we require a meta note and link to the canary run? Sahana: Added baseline.meta.txt and made the refresh script fail if run URL / benchmark-lab rev is missing. Also documented required approvals (Runtime + SRE). Marcus Reed: Scenario→tier mapping: where is it defined? I don’t want it diverging from benchmark-lab. Sahana: Mapping lives in config/tier_slo_gates.yaml but keys are scenario names produced by benchmark-lab. Added a small unit test to fail if unknown scenario appears without a mapping (forces update). Allison Grant: Please keep metric names consistent with observability-pack; also ensure we don’t explode labels in annotations. Sahana: Annotations only include tier + percentile + delta; no model×region fanout in the GitHub comment. CI failures during review: - 2026-03-12: lint failed (shellcheck on refresh_baseline.sh) → fixed quoting + set -euo pipefail - 2026-03-14: unit test flake on parser test due to unordered dict → normalized sort - 2026-03-16: workflow permissions issue uploading artifact → updated permissions: actions:read, contents:read, checks:write Final approvals: Sean Gallagher, Marcus Reed, Rafael Mendes. Merge: Squash merged to main after green canary run 2026-03-18 (run id: pc-20260318-1127).
checks: perf-canary tier gate (fast p99 / standard p95): pass

source.title: add adaptive outlier suppression to canary evaluator
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds an adaptive outlier suppression layer to the canary evaluator to reduce false-positive regression signals caused by transient noise. No user-facing API changes. Improves stability of rollout guards in bursty traffic patterns.
source.description:
Motivation: Canary evaluators occasionally trigger rollbacks due to short-lived outliers (e.g., transient downstream hiccups or probe skew) that do not reflect sustained regressions. This PR introduces an adaptive outlier suppression layer that reduces sensitivity to isolated spikes while preserving detection of real regressions.

Summary of changes: Implemented a three-stage suppression strategy integrated into the existing canary scoring pipeline: 1) lightweight per-probe z-score normalization (robust to small sample sizes), 2) median-filtered spike-dampening that discounts single-sample deviations based on recent windowed variance, and 3) Bayesian weight recalibration that downweights probes with recurring discordant signals across slices. The suppression is parameterized and feature-flagged so operators can tune aggressiveness per-customer or per-route.

Why this is safe: The suppression is applied only at the evaluator decision layer (after metric aggregation) and does not alter raw metric ingestion or storage. The default configuration is conservative (minimal suppression) and must be enabled per-environment via rollout flags. We added comprehensive unit and integration tests and a canary job to validate behavior under synthetic noise.

Commits (high-level): - Initial implementation of suppression module and unit tests - Integrate suppression into canary evaluator pipeline and expose feature flag - Add integration test harness and synthetic noise scenarios - Dashboard panels and runbook update - Tuning iteration after reviewer feedback

Files changed (high-level): - pkg/evaluator/suppression.go: core suppression algorithms + docstrings and benchmarks - pkg/evaluator/pipeline.go: hook points to call suppression before scoring - pkg/evaluator/suppression_test.go: unit tests covering edge cases and small sample behavior - integrations/noise-harness/: synthetic traffic generator and canary job config - docs/runbooks/canary-outlier-suppression.md: operational notes and tuning guide - dashboards/canary/suppression-overview.json: Grafana panel definitions summary - go.mod / go.sum: updated deps for probabilistic utils - scripts/ci/integration_canary.sh: new CI job to run noise scenarios

Behavioral details: - Default window: 5m sliding window, min samples=30 for full Bayesian recalibration. - For windows with <30 samples the module uses robust z-score thresholds to avoid overfitting. - The median-filter dampening multiplies a per-probe anomaly weight by (1 - alpha) where alpha adapts to observed variance; alpha in [0.0,0.5] by default. - Weight recalibration applies a Beta-prior update to probe reliability scores; probes that consistently disagree with other probes have their weight reduced smoothly rather than clipped.

Compatibility and rollout: - Feature-flagged under env CANARY_SUPPRESSION_MODE with values {off, conservative, aggressive}. - Default for hosted perf-canary is conservative to minimize behavioral drift. - Observability added: per-canary suppression_ratio and per-probe adjusted_weight metrics exported with same cardinality as existing metrics to avoid downstream changes.

Testing and CI: - Unit tests cover small-sample and large-sample regimes, edge-cases where all probes disagree, and reliability decay scenarios. - Integration test runs the synthetic noise harness that simulates 10k requests with injected 2-10s latency spikes and verifies no increase in false-positive rollback rate compared to baseline. - CI: initial run failed due to flaky integration timing; added retries and deterministic seed in harness; subsequent CI run passed (see CI summary below).

Performance: Benchmark micro-optimizations added to suppression.go to keep evaluator p95 CPU overhead < 1% on our standard canary worker. Local benchmarks included in package bench tags; results attached to PR artifacts.

Runbook: docs/runbooks/canary-outlier-suppression.md includes instructions for toggling the flag, recommended tuning for bursty vs. steady traffic, and how to inspect adjusted_weight and suppression_ratio metrics when diagnosing missed regressions.

Review thread (selected): - Oliver Grant: "Can we ensure this never masks a sustained 10% latency regression?" Author: "Yes — the Bayesian recalibration decays weights gradually; we also expose suppression_ratio across time. Conservative mode sets alpha max to 0.15 to keep sensitivity." - Priya Natarajan: "How does this interact with multivariate canaries (latency + error rate)?" Author: "Suppression runs per metric slice; we keep cross-metric correlation signals intact and only adjust probe weights per metric. Added a test case where error-rate and latency diverge." - Ethan Liu: "CI was flaky; add deterministic seed please." Author: "Added deterministic seed for the harness and retries; re-ran CI and green." - QA comment: "Add dashboard panels and add to SLO playbook." Author: "Included dashboard JSON and updated runbook; requested product review for dashboards."

CI summary: - unit: pass - lint: pass - integration-noise-harness: failed (first run) -> fixed timing, deterministic seed -> passed on rerun - e2e (canary pipeline): pass with conservative mode enabled in test cluster

Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main. Merge commit: "suppress: adaptive outlier suppression for canary evaluator (squash)". Changelog: internal perf-canary: improved false-positive resilience under noisy traffic.

Follow-ups and TODOs: - Monitor suppression_ratio for first 2 weeks in canary clusters (ENG-4128). - Add telemetry-based autotuning prototype that will adjust alpha based on traffic entropy (separate PR). - Consider exposing per-tenant defaults in Dedicated offering after initial observational period.

Related work: contrasts with previous probabilistic smoothing PRs by focusing on probe-weight recalibration and per-window conservative defaults rather than global smoothing kernels.

If reviewers want a walk-through, I can demo the noise harness and dashboard panels in the next engineering sync.
source.merge_commit: suppress: adaptive outlier suppression for canary evaluator (squash)

Review thread (selected): - Oliver Grant: \"Can we ensure this never masks a sustained 10% latency regression?\" Author: \"Yes — the Bayesian recalibration decays weights gradually; we also expose suppression_ratio across time. Conservative mode sets alpha max to 0.15 to keep sensitivity.\" - Priya Natarajan: \"How does this interact with multivariate canaries (latency + error rate)?\" Author: \"Suppression runs per metric slice; we keep cross-metric correlation signals intact and only adjust probe weights per metric. Added a test case where error-rate and latency diverge.\" - Ethan Liu: \"CI was flaky; add deterministic seed please.\" Author: \"Added deterministic seed for the harness and retries; re-ran CI and green.\" - QA comment: \"Add dashboard panels and add to SLO playbook.\" Author: \"Included dashboard JSON and updated runbook; requested product review for dashboards.\"

CI summary: - unit: pass - lint: pass - integration-noise-harness: failed (first run) -> fixed timing, deterministic seed -> passed on rerun - e2e (canary pipeline): pass with conservative mode enabled in test cluster

Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main. Merge commit: "suppress: adaptive outlier suppression for canary evaluator (squash)". Changelog: internal perf-canary: improved false-positive resilience under noisy traffic.

Follow-ups and TODOs: - Monitor suppression_ratio for first 2 weeks in canary clusters (ENG-4128). - Add telemetry-based autotuning prototype that will adjust alpha based on traffic entropy (separate PR). - Consider exposing per-tenant defaults in Dedicated offering after initial observational period.

Related work: contrasts with previous probabilistic smoothing PRs by focusing on probe-weight recalibration and per-window conservative defaults rather than global smoothing kernels.

If reviewers want a walk-through, I can demo the noise harness and dashboard panels in the next engineering sync.

source.title: add-rolling-entropy-degradation-fence
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a new entropy-based degradation fence to detect distributional degradation during canary rollouts; no behavioral changes to existing APIs. Ops: new alert and dashboard created.
source.description:
Summary: Introduces a Rolling Entropy Degradation Fence for canary rollouts. Motivation: percentile-based checks (p50/p95/p99) miss subtle distributional shifts where tails shift gradually or mass moves between latency modes. This PR implements an adaptive entropy-based detector that measures changes in the latency/throughput histogram distribution over a rolling window and fences rollouts when sustained distributional degradation is detected.

Design and behavior: The detector bins observed latency samples into an adaptive histogram (dynamic bucket sizing based on observed percentiles) and computes a rolling Shannon entropy plus a KL-divergence/JS-distance to a baseline histogram. We combine three signals: (1) instantaneous entropy change, (2) KL divergence to baseline, and (3) a sustained drift score computed with exponential decay. A canary fence is tripped only when the composite score exceeds the configured threshold for a configurable duration (default 5 minutes) and when traffic-weighted significance passes a minimal-sample check. The fence triggers: (a) pause rollout (canary traffic frozen), (b) escalate to oncall via alert channel, (c) automatically snapshot recent traces for post-mortem.

Implementation notes: - New module perf_canary/entropy_fence.py implements histogram maintenance, entropy/KL computation, smoothing and scoring. - A lightweight online estimator avoids storing raw samples; uses reservoir sampling for rare-event amplification in tail buckets. - Exposes config knobs via canon canary config: window_seconds, bucket_count, min_samples, entropy_threshold, kl_threshold, sustained_seconds. - Integration with existing rollout-control API: rollout_guard.plug('entropy_fence') returns decisions with reason codes and signal payload for observability. - Dashboard: dashboards/entropy_fence.json added with example panels (entropy timeseries, KL, composite score, sample counts). - Tests: unit + small integration tests under tests/test_entropy_fence.py and a synthetic load test script scripts/load/entropy_sims.py.

Compatibility & ops: Defaults are conservative (low false-positive rate). Gate-added to default canary guard chain but disabled for legacy customers via config flag. No schema changes to persisted canary objects. New IAM role not required.

Risks & mitigations: Entropy can be noisy on low-sample traffic; we added min_samples gating and sample-weighted smoothing. We also include a backoff and auto-unfreeze after manual-confirmation window if team marks event as false-positive.

Testing & validation: Local unit tests + simulated traffic scenarios included. We ran a two-week shadow run on internal staging (release/2026-02 staging) and verified zero false positives on normal traffic, detected 3 injected drift scenarios (gradual tail shift, bimodal shift, traffic-mode flip) and produced actionable signal.

Notes: This approach is complementary to existing percentile and trajectory detectors; recommended to use together for improved sensitivity to silent shifts.
commits: 8d3bf1a: initial implementation of entropy_fence module and API hook
b4c22e7: add unit tests, reservoir sampler, and smoothing code
c91f5d4: dashboard JSON and example configs
f2a7d58: integrate fence into rollout guard chain and add telemetry events
a1e9b62: add synthetic load simulator and CI job to validate behavior
d3c7a11: test flake fixes and doc clarifications
review_thread: Owen Patel (reviewer): The histogram bucket sizing looks good, but can we explain why we chose JS vs KL in the code comments? Requested a short rationale and a small refactor to make distance function pluggable.
Maya Chen (author): Added doc comment and made distance computation pluggable with default to JS for symmetry; pushed commit b4c22e7.
Liang Wu (reviewer): Please add a gating check for low-sample windows; entropy can spike on sparse traffic and we'd get noise in small tenants. Left a TODO that became a test request.
Maya Chen (author): Implemented min_samples gating, added unit test, and documented the default conservative thresholds. Pushed c91f5d4.

Priya Natarajan (reviewer): Integration with rollout-control API looks clean. One suggestion: expose the composite score in telemetry with reason codes for easier alert triage. Added a small nits list.
Maya Chen (author): Exposed composite_score and reason_code in telemetry events, updated dashboard panels, pushed f2a7d58.
CI system: integration-tests rerun required after test flake; reran and all checks passed.
Priya Natarajan (reviewer): Approve after CI green. Approved.
source.merge: Merged via squash on 2026-03-03 by Priya Natarajan (merge commit: squash: add entropy fence + tests). Merge strategy: squash-and-merge. Post-merge actions: CI promoted dashboard to grafana staging, created ops runbook entry, and scheduled 48h monitoring watch for rollout.

source.title: implement signal smoothing and traffic-granularity guard for noisy tail regressions
source.author: Asha Mehta
source.description:
Motivation: Recent canary runs showed recurrent false-positive tail-latency alerts during short traffic spikes, and we observed noisy variance for small-batch inference workloads. This PR introduces a two-part defense: 1) a lightweight temporal signal smoother that reduces high-frequency noise in per-route latency fingerprints while preserving changepoints, and 2) a traffic-granularity guard that slices canary evaluations by traffic orthogonals (route, payload-size bucket, and client tier) and gates rollouts when small, concentrated cohorts drive apparent regressions.

Goals: reduce alert churn, avoid unnecessary rollbacks for bursty micro-cohorts, and improve detection power for sustained regressions. This is intended as a conservative enhancement — default behavior is opt-in per-alert and the rollout guard has configurable thresholds and cooldown periods.

What changed (high-level): - Add exponential moving quantile smoother (EQS) and heteroskedasticity-aware window sizing to the canary metric stream processor. EQS adapts window length based on recent variance estimates so long-lived trends are preserved but transient spikes are suppressed. - Add traffic-granularity slicing to the canary evaluator. Evaluations now compute cohort-level fingerprints for route/payload_size/client_tier and will only escalate if either (a) a majority-weighted cohort shows sustained degradation, or (b) a global signal exceeds a tunable severity threshold. - New staged-fallback policy: on cohort-only degradation, the orchestrator applies a soft cork (reduce canary traffic by N%) and schedules a strengthened probe set; on global degradation the system triggers standard rollback. - Exposed config knobs in canary config and Redwood Console: smoothing_alpha, variance_thresholds, cohort_min_samples, staged_cork_percentage, staged_cork_cooldown. - Unit + perf tests and a small runbook for oncall to interpret cohort-level alerts.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of EQS and cohort slicing in canary processor
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing logic and cohort aggregation
- [x] Integration test with synthetic burst traffic profile
- [x] Console schema changes + docs entry
- [x] Runbook and alert text updates

Backward compatibility and safety: defaults preserve existing behavior when smoothing_alpha=0 and cohort_min_samples is large. The cohort guard is disabled by default for customers not enrolled in perf-canary v2.

Related: Linear ENG-1847 (reduce false positive canary alerts), ENG-1912 (cohort-aware canary rollouts), SUP-4123 (customer support ticket tying to bursty small-batch inference).
commits: feat(canary): add exponential quantile smoother with adaptive window sizing
feat(canary): compute cohort-level fingerprints by route/payload/client_tier
chore(tests): add synthetic burst integration profile and assertions
docs(console): add smoothing and cohort guard knobs to canary config page
fix(tuning): adjust default staged cork cooldown to 10m after QA

+}

review_comments: Ben Carter: Can we add telemetry for the effective alpha and window size so we can diagnose when smoothing hides regressions?
Asha Mehta (author): Added alpha/window metrics to canary.processor.metas in latest commit and wired them to the Console trend panel.
Linh Tran: Cohort_min_samples default looks high for low-traffic routes; can we lower but add a stricter majority-weight rule?
Asha Mehta (author): Lowered cohort_min_samples to 20 and added 'majority_weight_pct' param; updated tests and docs.
Priya Rao: Integration test was flaky locally due to timing; changed the synthetic profile to use deterministic timestamps — looks stable now. Approving after fixes.
Ben Carter: LGTM after telemetry + docs. Approving and requesting a squash merge.

source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash (maintainer: Ben Carter). Commit message: 'perf(canary): signal smoothing + cohort guard to reduce false-positive rollouts (ENG-1847, ENG-1912)'.
source.release_notes: Reduced canary alert churn by adding temporal smoothing and cohort-level gating. Admins can configure smoothing_alpha and cohort thresholds in the Canary config. Opt-in for perf-canary v2 customers; safe defaults preserve previous behavior.
source.notes_for_oncall: If a cohort alert fires, check canary.processor.metas.effective_alpha and cohort.sample_counts in the Console; if sample_counts < cohort_min_samples the alert may be noisy — consider extending cooldown or raising cohort_min_samples. For global escalations follow runbooks/canary-cohort-alerts.md.
source.impact_summary: Expected: fewer transient rollbacks for bursty workloads and lower alert noise. Monitored metrics: alert_churn_rate, false_positive_rate, time_to_rollback. No customer-facing API changes.
source.qa_steps: 1) Run integration-tests/profiles/burst_small_batch.json against canary local harness. 2) Toggle smoothing_alpha between 0 and 0.2 and verify changepoint detection on sustained 15% latency increase. 3) Validate cohort gating by replaying traffic concentrated in a single payload_size bucket and confirm staged cork behavior.
source.notes: This PR was intentionally conservative: smoothing is disabled by default for older canary configs and cohort gating requires explicit opt-in. We will monitor alert_churn_rate over the next two weeks post-merge.

source.title: Add stratified noise calibrator and adaptive probe scheduler for low-signal canaries
source.author: Anika Rao
source.description: Context: Our canary detector has been producing noisy alerts for low-volume / heterogenous traffic slices (short queries, long queries, browser vs API) leading to unnecessary rollbacks. Motivation: reduce false positives and speed up meaningful detection in low-signal cohorts without increasing probe cost. This PR introduces two complementary components: (1) Stratified Noise Calibrator (SNC) — a per-stratum robust noise estimator that derives baseline variance and Minimal Detectable Effect (MDE) using median-absolute-deviation + IQR trimming and an empirical noise model; and (2) Adaptive Probe Scheduler (APS) — a probe allocation layer that dynamically assigns probe budget to strata based on expected information gain (posterior variance reduction) and the user's rollout priority hints. Approach: SNC computes noise baselines per stratum (user_cohort, model_variant, sequence_length_bucket) and emits metrics (perf_canary.noise_baseline, perf_canary.mde_estimate). APS queries SNC and current traffic rates, computes expected value-of-information, then increments probe rates for strata where MDE > configured detection target or where posterior uncertainty dominates. Both pieces are integrated into the rollout guard: when aggregate confidence is below threshold, APS will increase probes up to a capped budget and, if uncertainty persists, the guard will hold or throttle the canary. Statistical protection: SNC applies Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across strata to control multi-comparison false alarms and exposes a confidence score to the guard. Implementation notes: core logic implemented in Go in go/perf/canary; metrics and alerts via Prometheus rules; simulator and integration scenarios in py/tools/canary_simulator to reproduce 0.5%–5% regressions per cohort. Tests: unit tests for SNC math, APS allocation policy, and a small-scale end-to-end simulation that runs in CI (simulated traffic with deterministic RNG seeds). Backwards compatibility: default behavior is off; rollout policy must opt-in via canary.config.alpha.enable_stratified_calibrator = true. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-5421) [x] unit tests [x] integration simulator scenarios [x] Prometheus recording rules and alert templates [x] docs and runbook [x] migration notes for existing canary dashboards. Risks & mitigations: metric cardinality — strata keys are canonicalized and capped to configured cardinality; noisy short-lived cohorts — APS applies minimum probe windows to avoid churn. Open questions: tune default MDE multiplier for PLG customers (left conservative).
commits: a1f3d7c: initial stratified noise calibrator core implementation
b2e5a8d: add adaptive probe scheduler + allocation algorithm
c3d9f02: expose prometheus metrics and recording rules
d4a0b6e: add unit tests for SNC and APS (edge cases)
e5f123a: add python simulator harness and sample scenarios
f6b7c9d: docs and runbook for calibration and ops playbook
g7c8d1e: follow-up fixes: zero-traffic guard, config defaults
review_comments: Miguel Torres (2026-02-21): Nice decomposition. Please make the MDE and FDR thresholds configurable and document the safe defaults. Request: add a low-cardinality mode for large clusters.
Author (Anika Rao) (2026-02-22): Added config keys canary.config.alpha.{mde_multiplier,fdr_q,cardinality_cap} and updated docs runbook with recommended defaults for PLG and Enterprise customers.

Priya Natarajan (2026-02-23): Concern about metrics cardinality — how do we prevent tag explosion from user_cohort? Recommendation: canonicalize cohorts + drop unknowns to "other". Also asked for an explicit test for zero-traffic strata.
Author (2026-02-24): Implemented canonicalization and added zero-traffic guard that returns no-op and logs metric; added unit test (noise_calibrator_test.go:TestZeroTrafficGuard).
Liam O'Shea (2026-02-25): LGTMed pending CI green; suggested small code cleanup for comment clarity in probe_scheduler.go.
Priya Natarajan (2026-02-26): Approved after CI rerun and minor doc tweak; merged by squash on 2026-02-27.
ci_checks: lint: pass (auto-format applied in commit g7c8d1e)
e2e-perf-sim: initial run failed due to flaky network-bind in CI container; rerun passed
source.merge_details: Squash merged by Priya Natarajan into main on 2026-02-27. Merge commit: squash a1f3d7c..g7c8d1e -> commit 9d2b4f1. Release note entry: 'perf-canary: add stratified noise calibrator and adaptive probe scheduler (opt-in feature)'.
source.release_notes: Added an opt-in stratified noise calibration and adaptive probe scheduler to improve canary detection in low-signal traffic. Operators must enable canary.config.alpha.enable_stratified_calibrator to use. Provides new Prometheus metrics perf_canary.noise_baseline and perf_canary.mde_estimate; includes runbook and dashboards.
source.notes_for_ops: Default disabled. If enabling in production, start with mde_multiplier=2.5 and cardinality_cap=50; monitor perf_canary.noise_baseline and perf_canary.probe_allocations and watch for increase in probe traffic. Use runbook docs/runbooks/canary-calibration.md for rollback steps.

Both pieces are integrated into the rollout guard: when aggregate confidence is below threshold, APS will increase probes up to a capped budget and, if uncertainty persists, the guard will hold or throttle the canary. Statistical protection: SNC applies Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across strata to control multi-comparison false alarms and exposes a confidence score to the guard. Implementation notes: core logic implemented in Go in go/perf/canary; metrics and alerts via Prometheus rules; simulator and integration scenarios in py/tools/canary_simulator to reproduce 0.5%–5% regressions per cohort. Tests: unit tests for SNC math, APS allocation policy, and a small-scale end-to-end simulation that runs in CI (simulated traffic with deterministic RNG seeds). Backwards compatibility: default behavior is off; rollout policy must opt-in via canary.config.alpha.enable_stratified_calibrator = true. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-5421) [x] unit tests [x] integration simulator scenarios [x] Prometheus recording rules and alert templates [x] docs and runbook [x] migration notes for existing canary dashboards. Risks & mitigations: metric cardinality — strata keys are canonicalized and capped to configured cardinality; noisy short-lived cohorts — APS applies minimum probe windows to avoid churn. Open questions: tune default MDE multiplier for PLG customers (left conservative).

source.title: Add latency-cliff detector and adaptive-concurrency rollout guard
source.author: Miguel Torres
source.description: Motivation: We've observed hard 'latency cliff' failure modes where p95/p99 jump sharply within a short window even though rolling averages look stable; these events bypass percentile-based canaries and lead to partial outages. This PR implements a latency-cliff detector that detects rapid percentile deltas across short windows and wires an adaptive-concurrency rollout guard that temporarily reduces per-replica concurrency and pauses rollouts when a cliff is detected. Changes include detector algorithm, integration with the canary evaluation pipeline, a lightweight local load harness for reproducible cliffs, dashboards, and unit/integration tests. Checklist: [x] detector algorithm and unit tests, [x] canary integration and gating, [x] adaptive-concurrency policy and actuator, [x] dashboards and runbook, [x] CI flake fixes. Rollout plan: rollout as a guarded feature flag for 2 weeks, enable in perf-canary staging, monitor metrics and KB/alerts, then enable for canary->prod rollouts. Backwards compatibility: no public API changes; the guard is opt-in via canary config; defaults are off. Performance cost: detector is O(1) per incoming metric update and uses bounded memory. Related: ENG-1428, ENG-1534. Test plan: unit tests cover algorithm edge cases; integration test injects synthetic cliff using examples/load-profiles/stepped-cliff.yaml; manual verification with dashboard in charts/perf-canary-dashboard.json.
source.commit_summary: 3 commits: add latency_cliff_detector with unit tests; connect detector to canary evaluator and alerting; add adaptive_concurrency_guard, actuator, integration tests, and dashboard updates
review_comments: Priya: Nice direction; can you limit the detector memory footprint and add a micro-benchmark?
Miguel: Added a sliding-window packed sketch and micro-benchmarks under perf-canary/tests/benchmarks; memory per-detector capped at 48KB - measurement attached.
Ethan: Request to rename metric labels to follow prometheus naming conventions (perf_canary_cliff_delta -> perf_canary_lat_cliff_delta).
Miguel: Renamed metric and updated dashboards, also added a compatibility shim for older dashboards.
Sana: Integration test is flaky on CI (timing); suggested increasing timeout and using deterministic seed. Miguel: increased timeout and added deterministic seed; CI rerun passed.
Priya: Approving after test stability confirmed.
ci_checks: integration-tests: fail on first run (timeout), rerun: pass
source.release_notes: Adds a latency-cliff detector that flags sudden percentile spikes and an adaptive concurrency rollout guard that reduces per-replica concurrency when cliffs are detected; includes dashboards, runbook, and a load profile example for reproducible tests. Opt-in guard; no breaking API changes.

source.title: Add stochastic concurrency fence and staggered probe scheduler to reduce probe-induced noise
source.author: Evan Cho
source.release_notes:
- Introduces a probabilistic concurrency fence that caps request concurrency with tunable drop probability to prevent burst amplification of tail latency.
- Adds a staggered probe scheduler that spreads canary probe traffic to reduce synchronization noise.
- New metric: perf_canary.concurrency_entropy (used by rollout guard).
- Backwards-compatible knobs; feature gated behind `perf.canary.stochastic_fence` flag.
source.description:
Context and motivation: We observed a recurring class of false-positive canary regressions when probe traffic arrives in short bursts or when client-side concurrency spikes align with canary probe windows. These cases amplify tail latency and create high variance in the canary signal, causing unnecessary rollbacks and developer churn. This PR implements two complementary mitigations: a stochastic concurrency fence (probabilistic admission control) to smooth bursty concurrent requests and a staggered probe scheduler to desynchronize probe timing across hosts.

What this changes (high level):
- Add perf-canary detector that computes `concurrency_entropy` per host and enables a probabilistic cap when entropy exceeds threshold.
- Replace existing probe dispatcher with a staggered scheduler that shards probe time windows to hosts and backfills missed probes without amplifying traffic.
- Export new prometheus metrics and tracing spans for debugging (see docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md).

Checklist: [x] design doc (perf-canary/docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md) [x] unit tests for fence and scheduler [x] integration stress test (tests/integ/canary_stress.sh) [x] backward-compatible config defaults [x] feature-flagged rollout in console

Implementation notes: The stochastic fence uses a lightweight token counter per runtime worker plus a small RNG-based drop decision to probabilistically reject new speculative requests once the measured concurrency distribution entropy rises above a configured baseline. The fence is parameterized by window_ms, entropy_threshold, and drop_p_max; drop probability grows with measured excess entropy to avoid hard cuts. The staggered probe scheduler assigns a deterministic offset per host based on host-id hashing and a jitter seed; it cooperates with the fence so probes don't get entirely rejected.

Safety and rollout: The feature is behind `perf.canary.stochastic_fence` flag and defaults to disabled in release builds. The canary pipeline now evaluates `perf_canary.concurrency_entropy` during pre-rollout shoulders and will auto-abort rollouts if entropy increases > 2x baseline and tail-p95 latency deviates > 20ms. We added a soft-fallback that disables the fence automatically if overall throughput drops > 5% relative to baseline.

Testing and benchmarks: Microbenchmarks show a 40% drop in variance for p99 under synthetic burst traffic and no measurable throughput regression (<1% difference) with default params. Integration stress runs ran for 2 hours across multiple hosts; one flaky e2e test failed initially due to an off-by-one in the offset calculation (fixed in commit 3) and CI was rerun.

Related: ENG-8421 (noise-reduction effort), ENG-8502 (canary signal stability). Design doc: perf-canary/docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md.

Please focus reviews on: correctness of entropy calc, safety of the fallback heuristics, and Prometheus naming/labels.

Summary of changes and commits below.
commits: c1f4d8a: initial scaffold for stochastic fence and scheduler (stubs + interfaces)
d2b7a10: implement concurrency entropy collector and gating logic; add unit tests
e3a9b21: finalize scheduler offset algorithm, fix offset off-by-one under host-id wrap (fix flake)
f4b2c33: docs, prometheus metrics, integration stress harness and release notes
review_comments: Priya Natarajan: 'Entropy calc looks reasonable, can you add a short comment describing numeric stability assumptions around log2? Also add a small unit test for empty-window behavior.'
Evan Cho (author): 'Addressed: added numeric-stability comment and an empty-window unit test in commit d2b7a10.'
Marcus Li: 'What happens to canary latency SLOs if we enable the fence with very aggressive drop_p_max? We should ensure the fallback disables fence before operator intervention.'
Evan Cho: 'Added soft-fallback thresholds (throughput delta check) and documented them in the config docs. See defaults.toml and design doc.'
Ana Gómez: 'Can we add a short grafana panel to the perf-canary dashboard to visualize concurrency_entropy alongside p95/p99?'
Evan Cho: 'Added panel suggestion to the design doc; dashboards will be included in a follow-up PR to avoid coupling the dashboard change to runtime.'

source.title: Add billing meter regression scenarios for streaming, retries, and fallback routing
source.author: Leila Farouk
source.body:
Motivation: We’ve had multiple meter-related regressions where request-level behaviors (stream disconnects, server-side retry/fallback, and client retry with idempotency) caused double-counting or inconsistent attribution between (a) usage events and (b) the billing ledger. Unit/integration tests in the monorepo cover code paths, but we were missing an end-to-end canary that validates metering invariants against a live stack (gateway -> inference -> usage event emitter -> aggregation/export) under realistic failure modes.

This PR adds new perf-canary scenarios that intentionally trigger streaming + retry + fallback behaviors and then validate the expected metering invariants by querying the usage API / internal usage-events view within a bounded time window.

What’s included (new scenarios): 1) streaming_disconnect_no_double_count: Start a streaming chat completion, consume first N chunks, then terminate the client connection. Expectation: billed_tokens matches tokens actually generated before disconnect; no second usage event is emitted during cleanup; request_count increments exactly once; retry_count is 0. 2) server_retry_fallback_single_bill: Force a server-side retry/fallback by routing the request through a fault-injection profile (first attempt returns 5xx from upstream; second attempt succeeds). Expectation: the request is billed once; usage_event_id is stable across attempts (or deduped via attempt_group_id); raw_tokens may reflect both attempts in internal debug fields but billed_tokens reflects only the successful attempt; retry_count == 1. 3) client_retry_idempotent_key_dedup: Send a request with an Idempotency-Key, intentionally time out client-side before completion, retry with same key. Expectation: either (a) second call returns cached result and no additional billing occurs or (b) second call resumes and still produces a single billable event; duplicate_event_rate stays 0 for this request_id. 4) fallback_routing_region_attribution: Trigger a policy-based fallback from primary region to secondary region (capacity constraint simulation). Expectation: billing uses the final served region; region dimension is present and consistent across usage event + usage export; no “split” billing across regions for a single request.

Meter invariants validated: - Exactly-once billing per idempotency scope: sum(billed_tokens) and count(distinct billable_request_id) do not increase with retries/fallback beyond the expected single billable unit. - Streaming semantics: billed_tokens are derived from generated content that was successfully produced/committed, not from partial transport artifacts. - Attribution completeness: region, model, account_id, and endpoint are non-null; fallback routing uses the served_region dimension. - Dedupe signals: duplicate_event == false for all scenario request_ids; attempt_group_id groups retries.

Implementation notes: - Adds a small “meter_assert” helper that polls the usage API for the scenario request_ids with exponential backoff (default 8 minutes) to account for async event processing. - Uses deterministic scenario tags appended to metadata (x-redwood-canary-scenario + x-redwood-canary-run-id) so Finance/Eng can locate runs in Snowflake/Grafana during incident triage. - Adds fault-injection hooks via existing canary config (no new prod dependencies). - Adds a lightweight JSON snapshot written to canary artifacts (expected vs observed totals) for debugging and historical comparison.

Rollout/ops: - These scenarios are initially gated behind CANARY_ENABLE_METER_SCENARIOS=true in staging and canary-prod. - Alerting is set to “warn-only” for 1 week (no paging) until we establish baseline flakiness and tune the polling window.

Related: - redwood/pr-27463 (fix double-counting on stream retry) - redwood/pr-27518 (shadow billing compare mode) - redwood/pr-27485 (usage API: billed_tokens + cache_hit fields) - ENG-4430 (bug: prevent double-counting on retry path) - PM-1872 (metering change control / shadow period requirement)

Testing: - Local: make test (scenario unit tests) - Staging: ran canary suite x3 with fault injection enabled; no invariant violations; max usage-event lag observed 3m12s. - CI: lint + go test + docker build pass.

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (1) invariant definitions (do we agree on billing semantics under streaming disconnect + retries), (2) polling/timeout defaults to avoid flake, and (3) scenario tagging fields to ensure they won’t leak to customer-visible metadata.
commits: canary: add meter_assert polling helper for usage API verification
scenarios: add streaming_disconnect_no_double_count
scenarios: add server_retry_fallback_single_bill with fault injection config
scenarios: add client_retry_idempotent_key_dedup
scenarios: add fallback_routing_region_attribution
ci: upload meter scenario snapshots as build artifacts
docs: add runbook snippet for meter scenario failures
notable_snippets: meter_assert invariant check (excerpt): assert.Equal(1, observed.DistinctBillableRequests) && assert.InDelta(expected.BilledTokens, observed.BilledTokens, expected.TokenTolerance)
checks: CI / scenario dry-run (no fault injection): pass
review_conversation: Nadia Rahman: For streaming_disconnect, are we sure billed_tokens should exclude tokens generated after client disconnect but before server notices? We need to align with Hosted API meter spec. Suggest tolerance window or explicit expectation based on commit semantics.

Leila Farouk (author): Good call. The scenario now asserts billed_tokens <= generated_tokens_observed_by_client + 1 chunk tolerance, and also requires only one billable event. I added a comment that this checks for “no double bill” and “bounded over-billing” rather than exact equality due to transport timing.

Logan Wright: Polling window at 8 minutes seems long; can we reduce to 5 to catch stuck pipelines faster? Also please ensure we’re not using customer-facing usage export endpoints for internal canaries.

Leila Farouk (author): Reduced default to 6 minutes with a per-scenario override. We’re using the internal canary service account against /internal/usage-events (not customer export). I added a guard to fail fast if configured to hit the public export route.
Aisha Bello: +1 on warn-only initially. Can you emit a single summary metric per scenario (pass/fail + observed lag) so Grafana can show trends and we can tune thresholds?
Leila Farouk (author): Added metrics canary_meter_scenario_pass{scenario=} and canary_meter_scenario_usage_lag_seconds{scenario=}. Snapshots still uploaded as artifacts for deep dives.

Cole Summers: In server_retry_fallback, please assert request_count stays 1 even when retry_count==1. That’s the key billing bug class. Also ensure idempotency scenario uses the same Idempotency-Key across attempts and captures both HTTP responses.

Leila Farouk (author): Updated assertions: request_count == 1, billed_request_count == 1, retry_count == 1; idempotency scenario now logs both response IDs and asserts they map to a single billable_request_id.
source.release_notes: Internal: Added perf-canary metering regression scenarios covering streaming disconnects, server/client retries, and fallback routing; warns on invariant violations and emits summary metrics for trend monitoring.

Related: - redwood/pr-27463 (fix double-counting on stream retry) - redwood/pr-27518 (shadow billing compare mode) - redwood/pr-27485 (usage API: billed_tokens + cache_hit fields) - ENG-4430 (bug: prevent double-counting on retry path) - PM-1872 (metering change control / shadow period requirement)

Testing: - Local: make test (scenario unit tests) - Staging: ran canary suite x3 with fault injection enabled; no invariant violations; max usage-event lag observed 3m12s. - CI: lint + go test + docker build pass.

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (1) invariant definitions (do we agree on billing semantics under streaming disconnect + retries), (2) polling/timeout defaults to avoid flake, and (3) scenario tagging fields to ensure they won’t leak to customer-visible metadata.

source.title: Introduce sequential probe compositor and adaptive cutover allocator
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body: Motivation: improve canary probe efficiency and reduce probe-induced noise during rollouts by composing sequential probes and allocating cutover budgets adaptively based on recent telemetry. This change introduces a Sequential Probe Compositor that stitches short-window synthetic probes into prioritized sequences to better surface phase-aligned regressions, and an Adaptive Cutover Allocator that computes per-rollout cutover budgets using traffic-conditioned stability scores and SLO-weighted credits. Key goals: lower false positives from probe collisions, preserve signal fidelity for tail latency, and enable safer gradual cutovers for large traffic shifts. Implementation notes: compositor merges probe windows across latency, throughput and error-rate signals; it maintains ordering and backpressure semantics so probes do not overload healthy replicas; allocator uses EWMA of percentile deltas and a stability-credit ledger to decide cutover percentages and cooldowns; both components expose Prometheus metrics and tracing hooks for downstream canary evaluators. Testing & rollout: added unit tests for sequence stitching and allocator math, integration harness that replays historical traffic traces to validate conservative/default budgets, and e2e traffic-driven scenarios validating fallback to existing sentinel policy. Checklist: [x] design doc referenced (ENG-2201); [x] unit + integration tests; [x] telemetry & dashboards; [x] docs/recipes update; [x] staged rollout to staging clusters. Related: ENG-2201, ENG-2283. This PR is a functional addition to perf-canary and is backwards-compatible with existing probe configs; default behavior remains unchanged until opt-in is enabled on rollout policies.

b4c2d19: implement AdaptiveCutoverAllocator with stability credit ledger
c7d9f02: expose new Prometheus metrics and tracing spans
d2e8a55: add integration harness replay scenarios and golden files
e9f1b6c: docs: add recipes section and rollout guidance
f3a6b7d: fix: address race in compositor ordering (review feedback)
review_comments: Liam Carter: Nice direction — can you clarify how the compositor handles overlapping probes from different cohorts? Suggest adding a determinism test for ordering.
Priya Natarajan: Added deterministic ordering test and documented tie-breaker (cohort priority then timestamp).
Aria Gomez: CI flaked on replay harness (timeout) — looks like the test fixture needs a longer deadline.
Priya Natarajan: Increased harness timeout and added retries; re-ran CI; see updated CI run below.
Marta Ivanova: Minor nit: allocator math uses EWMA window 60s by default — please make configurable per-deployment.
Priya Natarajan: Exposed ewma_window and stability_threshold as config flags with safe defaults.
Chen Wu: Approve after addressing race and adding metrics; verified dashboards in staging.
source.release_notes: Add Sequential Probe Compositor and Adaptive Cutover Allocator: opt-in canary features that reduce probe noise and compute conservative cutover budgets based on recent telemetry; metrics and recipes added to docs.

source.title: Add rolling resilience-curve canary with tiered fallback and rate fallthrough
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Introduces a rolling resilience-curve canary that detects gradual, sustained degradations and a tiered fallback policy (soft fallback -> model variant -> rate fallthrough) to limit blast radius during rollouts.
source.description: Motivation: We observed slow, multi-hour regressions that did not trigger existing spike-focused detectors; this PR adds a time-weighted 'resilience curve' canary that measures cumulative degradation slope across cohorts and triggers tiered safeguards. High level: 1) rolling resilience curve evaluator that computes a decay-weighted degradation area under curve (AUC) for latency and error rate across multiple cohort slices (region, client-class, route). 2) temporal quorum voting: requires consensus from multiple independent cohorts before a hard fallback. 3) tiered fallback policy: soft fallback (traffic shaping + alert only), model-variant fallback (route to a lower-cost stable variant), and rate fallthrough (graceful request shedding with progressive SLAs). 4) adaptive probe scheduling that increases probe frequency for cohorts showing rising AUC and backs off when stabilized. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-18721) - [x] unit + integration tests - [x] e2e smoke in perf lab - [x] docs added under docs/perf - [x] fallthrough behavior validated with canary simulator Implementation notes: The resilience curve is implemented in canary/engine/resilience_curve.py with an efficient incremental aggregator and configurable half-life. Voting is implemented as a quorum module that accepts weighted cohort votes. Fallbacks are implemented in alerts/rollout_guard.go and routing/soft_fallback.rs. This PR also adds a canary simulator harness and two smoke tests. Related: builds on prior probe-scheduling work (ENG-17234). Risk/rollback: feature gated behind canary.v2 flag; rollout guard defaults to soft mode until explicitly enabled in the console. Testing: see test/integration/resilience_smoke_test.py and perf-canary lab run id perf-lab-20260212-07. Merge notes: squash-merged to main after addressing review comments and CI fixes.

c1a7b88: implement temporal quorum voting and cohort weighting

alerts/rollout_guard.go: added TieredFallbackController with modes SOFT, VARIANT, RATE_FALLTHROUGH
routing/soft_fallback.rs: added route_fallback() that respects routing weights and inflight KV cache preservation
docs/perf/resilience_curve.md: contains math, operational knobs, and suggested thresholds for typical LLM workloads
review_conversation: Rafael Torres: Nice approach. Request: add unit tests for cohort weighting edge cases and a test demonstrating quorum behavior when one cohort flaps.
Maya Chen: Added tests for cohort weighting and simulated a single-cohort flap scenario in resilience_smoke_test.py (see latest commit).
Anita Singh: CI lint flagged formatting in resilience_curve.py and a potential atomicity issue in aggregator.inc(); please address.
Maya Chen: Fixed lint and switched aggregator counters to atomic primitives; pushed fix (e9a0f22).
Ethan Park: Recommend lowering default sensitivity for hosted tier to avoid noisy fallbacks; applied in config with docs update.
Anita Singh: Approved after fixes (LGTM).
Release-Engineering Bot: perf lab smoke run completed; 95th latency regression < 2% under synthetic burst; canary simulator validated tiered fallback behavior.

source.testing_instructions: Run tools/canary_simulator/sim_driver.py --scenario rising-latency to validate soft fallback and --enable-variant to test variant fallback. See docs/perf/resilience_curve.md for expected signals and thresholds.

source.title: Add TTFT/TPOT threshold profiles for FP8/INT8 kernel canaries (per-SKU baselines + noise suppression)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description: Context: As part of the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint, we’re enabling quantized kernel modes behind flags and rolling out via perf canary. The existing canary gating uses a single threshold policy that was tuned on baseline (FP16/BF16) runs and has been noisy for quantized modes (especially when sample sizes are small or when the scheduler mix shifts TTFT/TPOT differently). This PR introduces explicit threshold profiles for quantized kernels and per-SKU baselines so we can gate safely and reduce false positives while still catching real regressions. Summary of changes: (1) Add threshold profiles keyed by kernel_mode (baseline|fp8|int8) and sku (h100|a100|l4) with separate TTFT/TPOT constraints and minimum sample requirements. (2) Add noise suppression: a small-sample guardrail, robust aggregation using trimmed mean for p95 deltas, and an EWMA-based stability check that prevents flip-flopping when the signal is borderline. (3) Add a per-SKU baseline registry: baseline references are pinned by (model, sku, region, traffic_profile) so the canary compares fp8/int8 vs the correct baseline cohort rather than a global default. (4) Add explicit “capacity/variance” classification: if GPU clocks are not locked or if variance exceeds the configured bound, canary emits a WARN and does not auto-rollback (still pages on-call if error rate spikes). Default policies included (initial cut): H100 fp8 is allowed tighter TPOT deltas but slightly looser TTFT due to known launch/scheduler interactions in mixed workloads; A100 int8 is looser on TPOT until we finish shape guardrails coverage; L4 has stricter min-sample and longer evaluation window to smooth bursty workloads. Config format: new YAML profiles under config/threshold_profiles/ with schema validation in CI. Example snippet (illustrative only): profile: quantized_h100_fp8; ttft_p95_delta_pct_max: 6; tpot_p95_delta_pct_max: 3; min_requests: 2000; ewma_alpha: 0.2; trimmed_mean_pct: 10. Operational notes: This PR does NOT change any runtime flags. It only changes how perf-canary evaluates telemetry deltas when kernel_mode is fp8/int8. Rollback behavior: auto-rollback triggers remain enabled only when (a) min_requests met, (b) stability check passes (not high variance), and (c) delta breaches the configured bound for 2 consecutive windows. Otherwise we emit a blocking canary failure (no rollout progression) but do not auto-flip flags. Follow-ups: (a) once ENG-7428 matrix runs are complete we should tighten H100 fp8 TTFT; (b) add model-family overrides for known long-context TTFT sensitivity; (c) wire in the new metrics dimensions (model/gpu/kernel_mode) after runtime PR lands (tracked in ENG-7427/PR-19934 in monorepo).
commits: Add threshold profile schema + loader (validation, defaults, docs)
Introduce per-SKU baseline registry and cohort keying
Implement noise suppression (min sample, trimmed mean, EWMA stability)
Add quantized profiles (H100 FP8, A100 INT8, L4 INT8) + tests
Update evaluator output formatting and runbook notes
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes: The auto-rollback guardrails look good. Two questions: (1) what’s the behavior when sample size is met but variance is high? (2) can we ensure this doesn’t block non-quantized rollouts? I don’t want a global gating change that surprises us.

Allison Grant (author): (1) If variance > variance_max (computed from baseline window + current window), we classify the result as UNSTABLE and do not auto-rollback; we still stop progression and mark the canary as failed so on-call looks. (2) Non-quantized uses the existing default profile; the new profiles are selected only when kernel_mode tag matches fp8|int8. I added a unit test to ensure baseline profile remains the default when kernel_mode missing.

Noah Patel: The EWMA stability check is a good addition, but I’m concerned about the trimmed mean on p95 deltas — are we trimming across shards or across time windows? Also: can you link to the rationale from the sprint plan/variance investigation?

Allison Grant (author): Trim happens across time windows within the evaluation interval (we keep N windows, compute delta per window, then trim top/bottom 10% before aggregating). We don’t trim across shards; shards are already aggregated upstream. I added a comment + doc section and linked the approach to ENG-7429 notes in Linear.

Irene Choi: Please include explicit SKUs as they appear in fleet labels. Our H100 label is "h100-sxm" and "h100-pcie" in some regions. Can the profile system map both to "h100" without copy/pasting config?
Allison Grant (author): Added sku_aliases: [h100-sxm, h100-pcie] support in schema and mapped both to canonical "h100". Same for a100-80gb vs a100-40gb (canonical "a100"). Tests included.

Marcus Reed: For TTFT vs TPOT, the profile has TTFT looser than TPOT. That matches what we saw, but we should cap absolute TTFT delta in ms too; percent deltas can blow up on low-latency regions. Can we add an optional absolute bound?

Allison Grant (author): Added optional ttft_p95_delta_ms_max and tpot_p95_delta_ms_max fields. Set defaults only for L4 (since percent was too sensitive in our synthetic runs). Left H100/A100 on percent-only for now.
Rafael Mendes: Approved once the sku alias mapping lands.
Rafael Mendes: Looks good now. Approved.
source.release_notes: Perf-canary: Added quantized-kernel threshold profiles (FP8/INT8) with per-SKU baselines and noise suppression. Improves canary signal quality and reduces false-positive rollbacks during quantized rollouts.

source.title: introduce adaptive sensitivity scheduler for canary detectors
source.author: Eleanor Park
source.description:
Motivation: long-running canary evaluators are sensitive to traffic volatility and changing baseline variance; fixed detection thresholds either generate noisy false positives during churn or miss subtle regressions when traffic is stable. This PR implements an Adaptive Sensitivity Scheduler (ASS) that continuously tunes detector sensitivity based on short-term traffic variance, cohort churn, and historical false-positive rate.

What it does (high level): introduces a scheduler that adjusts detector thresholds and aggregation windows at runtime. The scheduler observes three signals: instantaneous request-rate volatility, percentile drift across 1/5/15-minute windows, and the detector's empirical false-positive count. Using a lightweight Bayesian update, it adapts the detection sensitivity multiplier and the sample window size with safety bounds to avoid oscillation. Integrations added: canary evaluator policy (gates sensitivity changes behind rollout guard), telemetry hooks for new metrics (sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec, scheduler_confidence), and opt-in feature-flagging via canary config.

Key design points: (1) Conservative priors: initial sensitivity is a small multiple of baseline to prevent overreaction after deploy spikes. (2) Stability hysteresis: changes are rate-limited and smoothed with exponential decay to avoid flip-flopping when traffic oscillates. (3) Safety gates: sensitivity cannot exceed an upper bound derived from SLO burn-rate budgets; if upper bound is reached scheduler forces immediate pause and raises a canary-alert event. (4) Auditability: scheduler emits human-readable decisions and rationale to the canary run log for postmortems.

Files & code: new scheduler implementation lives in perf-canary/scheduler/adaptive_scheduler.py with a lightweight C++-backed hot path for real-time metrics aggregation (perf-canary/scheduler/fastagg.cc) and Python bindings. Canary policy integrations are in perf-canary/evaluator/policy.py with new hooks to consult scheduled sensitivity before scoring. Configuration schema is extended: perf-canary/config/schema.yaml and charts/canary-helm/values.yaml include feature flag and bounds. Unit and integration tests added under tests/test_adaptive_scheduler.py and tests/integration/canary_scheduler_flow.test.

Testing and benchmarks: ran synthetic workloads (steady, bursty, seasonal) across canary clusters. Observed 47% reduction in false-positive rate on bursty traffic, with less than 3% decrease in true-positive detection rate for sustained regressions. Latency overhead on critical path measured <0.3ms per evaluation due to the fastagg hot path. Added CI job that runs targeted scheduler unit tests and a small end-to-end canary simulation (simulated 10k requests).

Rollout plan: feature gated off by default. Begin canary rollout to internal perf-canary staging clusters (10% of internal traffic) for 48h, monitor sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec and scheduler_confidence metrics. If metrics look stable (no increase in false-positive rate and no sustained sensitivity upper bound hits), enable for production read-only (telemetry only) then enable active mode after one week. Provided a rollback script and documented runbook in perf-canary/ops/runbooks/ASS-runbook.md.

Why unique: unlike prior work which focused on fixed heuristics or static traffic-shape profiles, this scheduler continuously adapts to observed variance and links sensitivity to SLO budget. It is tailored for real-world LLM inference traffic where short-lived bursts and long-tail shifts are common.

Notes: this PR intentionally keeps the scheduler implementation modular so it can be replaced with a learned policy later (see ENG-7402). Backwards compatibility: canary config defaults preserve existing behavior when the feature is disabled.
commits: feat(scheduler): add AdaptiveSensitivityScheduler core implementation
perf(scheduler): add fastagg.cc for low-latency metric aggregation and Python bindings
chore(config): extend schema.yaml and helm values for adaptive scheduler flags
test: add unit and integration tests for scheduler behavior
docs: add runbook and telemetry docs for adaptive scheduler
review_comments: Ava Chen: design looks solid — please add an explicit circuit-breaker metric when upper bound is hit so SRE can alert quickly.
Eleanor Park: added scheduler_circuit_break_count metric and alert template in ops/runbooks; PTAL.
Miguel Soto: can you explain why Bayesian update was chosen vs. EWMA? Concerned about cold-start sensitivity.
Eleanor Park: added rationale to code comments and tests that verify conservative cold-start priors; EWMA was evaluated but gave higher false-positive tail during bursty traffic in our sims.
Priya Nair: unit test flaked on CI once — appears to be timing-related in the fastagg binding; add a small backoff/retry in test harness.
Eleanor Park: fixed test harness and added deterministic seeding for aggregator tests. CI reran green.
approvals: Priya Nair approved after test fix
source.release_notes: Add Adaptive Sensitivity Scheduler to perf-canary: dynamic tuning of detection sensitivity based on traffic variance, with safety gates and telemetry. Feature is gated and disabled by default.

title: introduce adaptive sensitivity scheduler for canary detectors
preview:
Motivation: long-running canary evaluators are sensitive to traffic volatility and changing baseline variance; fixed detection thresholds either generate noisy false positives during churn or miss subtle regressions when traffic is stable. This PR implements an Adaptive Sensitivity Scheduler (ASS) that continuously tunes detector sensitivity based on short-term traffic variance, cohort churn, and historical false-positive rate.

What it does (high level): introduces a scheduler that adjusts detector thresholds and aggregation windows at runtime. The scheduler observes three signals: instantaneous request-rate volatility, percentile drift across 1/5/15-minute windows, and the detector's empirical false-positive count. Using a lightweight Bayesian update, it adapts the detection sensitivity multiplier and the sample window size with safety bounds to avoid oscillation. Integrations added: canary evaluator policy (gates sensitivity changes behind rollout guard), telemetry hooks for new metrics (sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec, scheduler_confidence), and opt-in feature-flagging via canary config.

Key design points: (1) Conservative priors: initial sensitivity is a small multiple of baseline to prevent overreaction after deploy spikes. (2) Stability hysteresis: changes are rate-limited and smoothed with exponential decay to avoid flip-flopping when traffic oscillates. (3) Safety gates: sensitivity cannot exceed an upper bound derived from SLO burn-rate budgets; if upper bound is reached scheduler forces immediate pause and raises a canary-alert event. (4) Auditability: scheduler emits human-readable decisions and rationale to the canary run log for postmortems.

Files & code: new scheduler implementation lives in perf-canary/scheduler/adaptive_scheduler.py with a lightweight C++-backed hot path for real-time metrics aggregation (perf-canary/scheduler/fastagg.cc) and Python bindings. Canary policy integrations are in perf-canary/evaluator/policy.py with new hooks to consult scheduled sensitivity before scoring. Configuration schema is extended: perf-canary/config/schema.yaml and charts/canary-helm/values.yaml include feature flag and bounds. Unit and integration tests added under tests/test_adaptive_scheduler.py and tests/integration/canary_scheduler_flow.test.

Testing and benchmarks: ran synthetic workloads (steady, bursty, seasonal) across canary clusters. Observed 47% reduction in false-positive rate on bursty traffic, with less than 3% decrease in true-positive detection rate for sustained regressions. Latency overhead on critical path measured <0.3ms per evaluation due to the fastagg hot path. Added CI job that runs targeted scheduler unit tests and a small end-to-end canary simulation (simulated 10k requests).

Rollout plan: feature gated off by default. Begin canary rollout to internal perf-canary staging clusters (10% of internal traffic) for 48h, monitor sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec and scheduler_confidence metrics. If metrics look stable (no increase in false-positive rate and no sustained sensitivity upper bound hits), enable for production read-only (telemetry only) then enable active mode after one week. Provided a rollback script and documented runbook in perf-canary/ops/runbooks/ASS-runbook.md.

Why unique: unlike prior work which focused on fixed heuristics or static traffic-shape profiles, this scheduler continuously adapts to observed variance and links sensitivity to SLO budget. It is tailored for real-world LLM inference traffic where short-lived bursts and long-tail shifts are common.

Notes: this PR intentionally keeps the scheduler implementation modular so it can be replaced with a learned policy later (see ENG-7402). Backwards compatibility: canary config defaults preserve existing behavior when the feature is disabled.

source.title: Add quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput fence and severity scoring
source.author: Jonah Park
source.description:
Problem: Existing canary detectors surface aggregate latency shifts but often miss multi-phase regressions where different traffic slices degrade at different times. We need a detector that (1) understands traffic phase boundaries (time-of-day, model warmup phases, routing slices) and (2) produces a single severity score that reflects both quantile skew and the persistence of the shift so rollout guards can make informed decisions.

This PR introduces a quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput fence and a severity scoring function that folds into the canary evaluator pipeline. The detector computes quantile deltas per phase window and applies an exponential decay weighting across recent windows to emphasize sustained regressions while suppressing short-lived bursts. The severity score is a monotonic mapping of weighted-quantile deltas to [0,100] and is used by policy engines to trigger soft-fallback, pause, or hard-rollback depending on thresholds.

Key design points:
- Phase detection: configurable sliding window with heuristic phase boundary markers (traffic-rate change, model variant switch, time-partitioning). Phase rules are pluggable and default to traffic-rate heuristic.
- Quantile weighting: compare target percentiles (p50/p90/p99) against baseline, compute signed deltas, and transform them with a per-quantile sensitivity factor; combine into a single delta vector.
- Temporal aggregation: recent phase deltas are aggregated with exponential decay alpha (configurable) to compute sustained score.
- Severity mapping: configurable breakpoints to map numeric score to severity levels (info/warning/critical).
- Integration: detector implements the Detector interface and plugs into the existing canary evaluator; no changes required to routing or rollout policies other than optional configuration knobs.

Why different: unlike percentile-fusion detectors that treat traffic as a single homogeneous stream, this approach explicitly segments by phase and weights quantile violations to produce a severity that more closely tracks user-visible impact and avoids noisy short-lived triggers.

Behavioral knobs added in values and config:
- phase_window_seconds (default 300)
- lookback_phase_count (default 8)
- decay_alpha (default 0.4)
- quantile_weights: map {50:0.2, 90:0.5, 99:0.3}
- severity_thresholds: {warning: 25, critical: 60}

Testing and verification:
- unit tests for quantile delta calc, decay aggregation, and severity mapping included.
- local canary lab harness run against synthetic traffic with phase shifts (see test fixtures) demonstrating >92% detection rate for sustained 30% p90 regressions and <6% false positive rate on bursty noise.
- performance: detector adds ~1.3% CPU overhead in our canary agent under baseline load and 4MB additional RSS per agent.

Migration: default configuration preserves current canary behavior; operators can opt-in by enabling the detector through perf-canary config. Documentation added (docs/design/quantile-weighted-detector.md) and chart values exposed for helm deployments.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implementation of detector and integration with evaluator pipeline
- [x] Unit tests and CI coverage
- [x] End-to-end canary lab runs with synthetic traffic profiles
- [x] Documentation and helm values
- [x] Telemetry: new metrics prefixed with perf_canary.qw_ for score, phase_count, and per-quantile-delta

Related: SUP-4012 incident follow-up that motivated phase awareness; ENG-2145 for long-running canary reliability improvements.

Notes: This change is additive and opt-in. I expect additional tuning of quantile_weights and decay_alpha after broader rollout.
commits: a8d3f7f - implement quantile-weighted detector core and interface wiring (Jonah Park)
b2e9ae1 - add unit tests and synthetic fixtures for multi-phase traffic (Jonah Park)
c7f1d40 - expose helm values and add docs/design/quantile-weighted-detector.md (Jonah Park)
review_comments: Ava Chen (2026-02-11): Nice work overall. Two requests: (1) please add a short-circuit when phase_count < 2 to avoid noisy early scoring, (2) clarify metric cardinality implications in telemetry section.
Jonah Park (2026-02-11): Added short-circuit and added telemetry note; updated tests to cover the early exit case.

Diego Martinez (2026-02-13): Implementation looks solid. Concern about default decay_alpha=0.4 being too aggressive for weekend traffic patterns. Suggest 0.2 as a safer default and a migration note for ops teams.
Jonah Park (2026-02-14): Lowered default decay_alpha to 0.3 after offline sensitivity analysis and added guidance in docs about tuning for low-traffic windows.
Priya Nair (2026-02-16): Unit tests good. Please add an e2e assertion in canary lab harness that the detector emits a critical severity when synthetic 40% p90 shift sustained for 3 phases.
Jonah Park (2026-02-17): Added e2e assertion and updated synthetic fixture to include a 3-phase sustained shift scenario. CI re-run green.
ci_checks: CI / unit-tests: failed on first run due to race in test fixture, rerun after fix
CI / e2e-canary-lab: pass (includes new phase-shift scenario)
source.release_notes: Add quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput detector: opt-in canary detector that produces a severity score for phased regressions. Exposes helm values for tuning (phase window, decay alpha, quantile weights).
