source.title: Add Quality Gate enforcement check stage to perf-canary pipeline
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.description:
Background: Optimize now requires quality gates before applying cost-saving config diffs (batching/cache/quantization). We’ve had a near-miss during beta where enforcement drifted between services (Optimize apply endpoint blocked correctly, rollout promotion didn’t). This PR adds a dedicated perf-canary stage that continuously verifies gate enforcement semantics end-to-end against a controlled staging route, and alerts on regressions.

What this adds:
- New canary stage: `quality_gate_enforcement`
  - Executes an end-to-end scenario against staging Optimize + eval-harness + rollout-controller API surface.
  - Validates enforcement at the apply boundary (blocked when gate != PASSED) and at promotion boundary (canary promotion blocked on failed gate).
- A small, deterministic test fixture:
  - Uses a dedicated canary account + route (non-production) and a synthetic config diff that is expected to FAIL the gate (known-bad quant profile on long-context prompts).
  - Uses a separate config diff that is expected to PASS smoke gates (benign batching window tweak) to ensure the stage itself isn’t only testing the fail path.
- Result reporting:
  - Emits `perf_canary_stage_result{stage="quality_gate_enforcement"}` and per-assertion tags (apply_blocked, promotion_blocked, pass_path_ok).
  - Posts a structured summary to #eng-sre-canaries when failures persist > 2 runs, with links to gate run ids and artifacts.

Behavior / Failure modes:
- Stage is non-flaky by design: retries only on 5xx/timeouts; no retries on assertion failures.
- Hard timeout: 12m for the stage (to avoid blocking the entire canary suite). If evals exceed budget we treat as failure and page as SLO regression (this catches the "gates blocking rollouts too long" class of issues).
- Guardrails: stage aborts early if canary environment is not "staging" or if the route/account id doesn’t match the allowlist.

Rollout:
- Enabled in staging canary pipeline immediately.
- For prod canary pipeline, staged behind `CANARY_ENABLE_QUALITY_GATE_STAGE=true` (default off) so SRE can enable after one week of stable staging signal.

Notes:
- This PR does not change gating logic; it only validates existing behavior and surfaces regressions earlier.
- Related: ENG-3928 (rollout controller respects gate), ENG-3926 (metrics), ENG-3931 (required checks).

Screenshots/Artifacts:
- Example success run output is included in the PR comments (log excerpt + metric names).
commits: b8c0adf canary: add quality gate enforcement stage skeleton + wiring
7f2d6b1 canary: implement apply-blocked and promotion-blocked assertions
c2a19d0 canary: add allowlist guardrails + staging-only safety checks
9b7fcae observability: emit stage metrics + structured failure context
0e3f4d1 notifications: slack payload + dedupe for persistent failures
f4a1a22 docs: update runbook link + pipeline stage documentation
changed_files: pipelines/canary.yaml (add quality_gate_enforcement stage; feature flag in prod pipeline)
clients/rollout_controller_client.py (new helper: attempt_promotion + parse denial reason)
notifications/slack.py (new message template; persistent failure dedupe)
docs/canary-stages.md (stage description, timeouts, how to debug)
review_conversation:
Allison Grant: Metrics look good, but can we avoid high-cardinality tags (gate_run_id) on the main counter? Suggest logging gate_run_id and keeping metrics low-cardinality.
Connor O'Brien: Good call. Updated to emit gate_run_id only in logs + Slack payload; metrics now only tag stage/assertion/result/region.

Sean Gallagher: I’m worried about flakiness if the stage depends on a real eval run every time. Can we make the PASS path a smoke gate only (small prompt subset) and require eval runtime budget checks?
Connor O'Brien: Updated PASS path to use smoke-gate-only recommendation type; also added explicit budget assertion (fail if eval > 8m).

Sahana Iyer: Please add a guard to prevent this from ever running outside staging unless explicitly enabled. We’ve had canary fixtures leak into prod before.
Connor O'Brien: Added env allowlist + required flag `CANARY_ENABLE_QUALITY_GATE_STAGE=true` for prod pipeline; also fails closed if env cannot be verified.

Chloe Martin: On the promotion-blocked assertion: ensure we’re checking the denial reason code, not string matching. The rollout controller has structured errors.
Connor O'Brien: Switched to structured `denial_reason_code == QUALITY_GATE_NOT_PASSED`; added unit test for error parsing.

Sean Gallagher: Can we link the oncall runbook in the Slack message? Also add a dedupe window so we don’t spam when staging is degraded.
Connor O'Brien: Added runbook link to docs section and Slack payload; dedupe window set to 60 minutes per stage+region.
source.release_notes: Added a new perf-canary pipeline stage that continuously verifies Optimize quality gate enforcement (apply blocked unless PASSED; canary promotion blocked on failure) and alerts SRE on regressions. Staging enabled by default; prod gated behind feature flag.

source.title: Add representative workload suite to nightly canary + gating mode for release branches
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.body: Context: As part of the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project, we need the representative suite to run continuously (nightly) and also to be usable as a release gate for runtime release branches. This PR wires the representative suite runner into perf-canary with two execution modes: (1) nightly mode (signal + trend) and (2) gating mode (pass/fail) intended for release/* branches. Summary of changes: - Adds a new job definition for the representative suite (chat streaming, long-context prefill, embeddings high-QPS; tool-calling is stubbed in config but not enabled by default yet). - Introduces a `mode` flag: `nightly` vs `gating`. Nightly publishes full metric payloads and trends; gating evaluates a smaller set of canonical metrics against threshold config and emits a single PASS/FAIL with links to artifacts. - Adds threshold configuration files keyed by (model_family, workload, gpu_sku, region) with explicit metric boundaries: TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, throughput (tokens/s or req/s for embeddings), plus guardrail saturation signals. - Adds result reporting integration: JSON summary + a markdown report posted to the run output (and to the existing perf-canary comment bot when invoked from a PR workflow). - Adds a “compare-to-baseline” helper that reads the baseline anchor from the run manifest (per ADR-0172) and computes deltas with a simple noise model (min sample size + absolute and relative thresholds). - Adds routing behavior: nightly runs post to #perf-canary (non-paging); gating runs post to #perf-triage (non-paging) and only page on repeated failures (3 consecutive gating failures on the same release branch + same cohort). Important operational notes: - Gating is intentionally fail-closed for release branches (i.e., it will block promotion) but has an explicit override mechanism via a signed config exception (see `docs/gating-exceptions.md`). - We treat missing baseline for a cohort as “SKIP” (non-blocking) to avoid halting releases when a new cohort is added before the baseline sweep catches up. - Cold vs warm cache: gating evaluates both when available; nightly always reports both. By default, gating fails only on cold regressions (warm regressions are warnings) until we finish calibrating cache variance across SKUs. Related: - Linear: ENG-4828 - Confluence: Perf regression policy & thresholds (company standard) - ADR-0172 run manifest schema (consumed by this runner) Testing: - Added unit tests for threshold parsing + delta evaluation. - Ran a dry-run on us-east/H100 with a known-good baseline anchor; verified PASS in gating mode and successful artifact upload. - Simulated a regression by pinning an older baseline + forcing a degraded threshold; verified FAIL output and Slack routing. Rollout plan: - Enable nightly representative suite runs first (starting next scheduled window) and observe variance panels for 1 week. - Enable gating mode for `release/runtime-*` branches after Sana/Sean confirm calibrated thresholds for H100 + A100 cohorts. - Expand to eu-west once baseline sweep completes for that region. Notes / TODO: - Tool/function-calling workload is present in config but not scheduled; will land separately once the generator stabilizes. - Some cohorts are commented out pending L40S baseline completion.

perf-canary: implement nightly vs gating mode switch + CLI flags
thresholds: add initial q1-2026 representative suite threshold config (us-east H100/A100)
reporting: add markdown summary + artifact links (manifest, metrics, traces pointer)
eval: add delta computation + minimum sample size enforcement
docs: add gating exceptions policy and dry-run instructions
tests: add parsing + evaluation unit tests for thresholds
conversation: Sean Gallagher (review): Nightly vs gating separation looks good. Two concerns: (1) paging behavior and (2) what happens when baseline data is missing. We should not page on the first week of rollout, and missing baseline should not block release. Also, can we add a link to the run manifest in the summary so oncall can drill into env capture quickly?

Connor O'Brien (author): Agreed. Updated Slack routing so nothing pages by default; only repeated failures page (3 consecutive) and only for release branches. Missing baseline now yields SKIP (non-blocking) with an explicit log line and report section. Added manifest link to both JSON and markdown report.

Sana Farid (review): Please tighten the evaluator: right now it’s purely relative delta. For TTFT at low values, relative-only can be noisy. Can we do relative OR absolute thresholds (i.e., pass if delta < max(abs_ms, rel_pct))? Also enforce a minimum N for p99.

Connor O'Brien (author): Implemented `max(abs, rel)` rule per metric. Added `min_samples` per cohort and a hard floor for p99 evaluation. Unit tests added; see `internal/thresholds/evaluator_test.go`.

Rina Kobayashi (review): Config format is close to what benchmark-lab emits, but the workload naming needs to match the suite keys (chat_streaming vs chat-streaming). Otherwise we’ll end up with silent SKIPs. Also, please ensure the report includes the workload seed so we can re-run the same shape.

Connor O'Brien (author): Normalized workload keys to snake_case across suite + thresholds. Added validation to fail the job if a threshold file references an unknown workload key. Report now includes `workload_seed` from the manifest and prints the suite revision string.

Allison Grant (review): Please align metric names/tags with the metrics dictionary draft: TTFT/TPOT should be explicitly “server-side” and exclude client read time. Can you add a note to the docs and ensure the report uses those names verbatim?

Connor O'Brien (author): Updated report labels to `ttft_server_ms` and `tpot_server_ms` and added a short measurement boundary note in `docs/representative-suite-ops.md` referencing the shared definitions.
Sofia Ivanova (review): For gating mode, can we include queue wait and batch assembly time as secondary signals? Not for pass/fail yet, but printed as context to speed triage.
Connor O'Brien (author): Added a “diagnostics” section to the markdown summary that prints queue_wait_p95_ms and batch_assembly_p95_ms when present in the metrics payload (non-blocking).
Sean Gallagher (approval): Looks good with the routing tweaks + SKIP behavior. Approved after latest CI is green.
Sana Farid (approval): Threshold evaluator logic now matches the policy doc expectations. Approving.

title: Add representative workload suite to nightly canary + gating mode for release branches
preview: Context: As part of the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project, we need the representative suite to run continuously (nightly) and also to be usable as a release gate for runtime release branches. This PR wires the representative suite runner into perf-canary with two execution modes: (1) nightly mode (signal + trend) and (2) gating mode (pass/fail) intended for release/* branches. Summary of changes: - Adds a new job definition for the representative suite (chat streaming, long-context prefill, embeddings high-QPS; tool-calling is stubbed in config but not enabled by default yet). - Introduces a `mode` flag: `nightly` vs `gating`. Nightly publishes full metric payloads and trends; gating evaluates a smaller set of canonical metrics against threshold config and emits a single PASS/FAIL with links to artifacts. - Adds threshold configuration files keyed by (model_family, workload, gpu_sku, region) with explicit metric boundaries: TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, throughput (tokens/s or req/s for embeddings), plus guardrail saturation signals. - Adds result reporting integration: JSON summary + a markdown report posted to the run output (and to the existing perf-canary comment bot when invoked from a PR workflow). - Adds a “compare-to-baseline” helper that reads the baseline anchor from the run manifest (per ADR-0172) and computes deltas with a simple noise model (min sample size + absolute and relative thresholds). - Adds routing behavior: nightly runs post to #perf-canary (non-paging); gating runs post to #perf-triage (non-paging) and only page on repeated failures (3 consecutive gating failures on the same release branch + same cohort). Important operational notes: - Gating is intentionally fail-closed for release branches (i.e., it will block promotion) but has an explicit override mechanism via a signed config exception (see `docs/gating-exceptions.md`). - We treat missing baseline for a cohort as “SKIP” (non-blocking) to avoid halting releases when a new cohort is added before the baseline sweep catches up. - Cold vs warm cache: gating evaluates both when available; nightly always reports both. By default, gating fails only on cold regressions (warm regressions are warnings) until we finish calibrating cache variance across SKUs. Related: - Linear: ENG-4828 - Confluence: Perf regression policy & thresholds (company standard) - ADR-0172 run manifest schema (consumed by this runner) Testing: - Added unit tests for threshold parsing + delta evaluation. - Ran a dry-run on us-east/H100 with a known-good baseline anchor; verified PASS in gating mode and successful artifact upload. - Simulated a regression by pinning an older baseline + forcing a degraded threshold; verified FAIL output and Slack routing. Rollout plan: - Enable nightly representative suite runs first (starting next scheduled window) and observe variance panels for 1 week. - Enable gating mode for `release/runtime-*` branches after Sana/Sean confirm calibrated thresholds for H100 + A100 cohorts. - Expand to eu-west once baseline sweep completes for that region. Notes / TODO: - Tool/function-calling workload is present in config but not scheduled; will land separately once the generator stabilizes. - Some cohorts are commented out pending L40S baseline completion.

source.title: Shadow traffic overhead benchmark suite (routing mirror + telemetry) for go/no-go gates
source.author: Vikram Singh
source.release_notes: Adds a reproducible benchmark suite for measuring user-path overhead of shadow traffic mirroring across non-streaming + streaming workloads; produces gate-ready summaries (p50/p95/p99 delta, CPU/mem, telemetry overhead, and circuit-breaker behavior).
source.description:
Context: As part of Shadow Traffic (quality eval metrics) we need a repeatable way to quantify overhead from (a) request mirroring to shadow targets, (b) additional telemetry emission, and (c) backpressure/circuit breaker behavior under scoring/ingestion degradation. This suite is intended to run as a go/no-go gate for beta/GA (and as a nightly regression check).

What this PR does: 1) Adds a benchmark harness to perf-canary that drives realistic inference traffic (chat + streaming chat) through the Redwood gateway with shadowing enabled/disabled and varying sampling rates. 2) Captures user-path latency deltas and resource overhead while ensuring fail-open semantics (shadow path failures must not move user-path SLOs). 3) Emits a standardized results bundle (JSON + markdown) for consumption in rollout readiness docs and for CI gating.

Scenarios included: - Chat (non-streaming): 256 and 1024 token outputs, fixed prompt distribution; measures p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency, gateway CPU, and request error rates. - Streaming chat: measures time-to-first-token (TTFT), inter-chunk cadence, and tail latency; validates partial-chunk handling when shadow timeout trips. - Shadow sampling rates: 0% (baseline), 1%, 5%, 10%. - Shadow target behaviors: healthy, slow (adds 150ms), erroring (5xx), and unavailable (connection refused). - Telemetry modes: control-only vs control+shadow pair events (cardinality-safe dimensions only).

Go/no-go thresholds (current defaults; tunable via config): - User-path p99 delta <= +3ms at 1% sampling and <= +8ms at 10% sampling (non-streaming), measured at steady state (post-warmup). - Streaming TTFT p99 delta <= +5ms at 1% sampling and <= +12ms at 10% sampling. - Gateway CPU increase <= +5% absolute at 10% sampling under the reference load (see configs). - No statistically significant increase in user-path 5xx/timeout rate when shadow target is failing; circuit breaker should trip and shadow requests should be dropped while user path remains stable.

Outputs: - artifacts/shadow_overhead/<run_id>/results.json (machine-readable) - artifacts/shadow_overhead/<run_id>/summary.md (human-readable) - optional Prometheus snapshot export for deeper debugging

Notes: - This suite intentionally does not validate quality metrics correctness; it only measures performance/overhead and safety behavior. - Test prompts are synthetic and contain no customer data. - The benchmark defaults are aligned with the current routing implementation (mirroring inline with strict timeouts) and the SRE fail-open requirements.

Follow-ups: - Integrate this suite into the release-engineering feature flag plan as a required gate for enabling shadow traffic beyond internal tenants (tracked in ENG-5412). - Add an embeddings workload scenario once the shadow path supports embeddings consistently (out of scope for this PR).
commits: perf-canary: add shadow overhead benchmark harness (runner + configs)
bench: add streaming TTFT instrumentation and chunk cadence metrics
bench: add shadow-target fault injection (slow/error/unavailable)
ci: add nightly job + artifact upload for shadow_overhead suite
docs: add benchmark readme and interpretation guidance
source.checks: CI summary: ✅ build (linux/amd64) | ✅ unit tests | ✅ lint/format | ✅ PR smoke run (shadow_overhead @ 1% sampling, 3m) | ✅ artifact upload (PR) | ✅ dashboard JSON validation. Notable rerun: the first PR smoke run failed due to missing PERF_CANARY_TOKEN on forks; updated workflow to skip artifact upload on external contexts and reran successfully.
source.conversation:
Allison Grant (review): The headline metrics look right, but we need to be careful about label/cardinality in the exported results. Can you confirm we don’t include route_id/customer_id as raw strings? Also please add a section in README on how to compare runs (same prompt distribution + fixed seed).

Vikram Singh (author): Good call. Updated reporting to bucket route into {"route_class": "chat"|"streaming"} and to hash run_id only. Added fixed RNG seed + prompt distribution checksum and documented comparison steps in README.

Sean Gallagher (review): Two asks from SRE side: (1) warmup must be explicit (we’ve seen KV cache warmup skew p99 deltas) and (2) we should validate fail-open when shadow target is slow/erroring. Right now it measures but doesn’t assert.

Vikram Singh (author): Added a warmup phase (default 90s) that is excluded from results. Also added hard assertions: user-path timeout/5xx deltas must stay within configured bounds; if exceeded, the run fails and the summary highlights which gate failed. For fail-open, added fault injection modes and a circuit-breaker expectation check (shadow drop rate increases, user error rate stable).

Mina Haddad (review): Streaming: can we measure TTFT separately from completion? The routing change for streaming shadowing can impact TTFT even if overall completion is unchanged.

Vikram Singh (author): Yep—added TTFT histograms plus inter-chunk cadence percentiles. Summary now reports TTFT p50/p95/p99 deltas as the primary streaming gate.

Logan Wright (review): Please ensure we don’t rely on telemetry ingestion being healthy for benchmark correctness. The suite should still run if telemetry is lagging; we only measure overhead on user path.

Vikram Singh (author): Adjusted: telemetry collection is best-effort; benchmark uses gateway-side timers as source of truth. Telemetry export is optional and only included if available; it does not fail the run.

Cole Summers (review): For quotas/guardrails, add a note that the benchmark runs with elevated test tenant quotas, and link to the config field. Also confirm shadow requests are excluded from customer usage accounting in this setup.

Vikram Singh (author): Added README notes: suite uses the perf-canary tenant with separate accounting. Shadow requests are tagged with x-redwood-shadow: true and excluded from usage metering in the perf environment.

Final approvals: Allison ✅ Sean ✅ Mina ✅ Logan ✅
source.metadata_notes: PR intended as a gating signal for Shadow Traffic beta; results referenced in rollout readiness checklist. Suite is designed to be reproducible: fixed seed, fixed prompt distribution checksum, warmup exclusion, and explicit thresholds in config.

source.title: Add baseline fetch + regression evaluator (min samples, rolling window, pct thresholds)
source.author: Noah Patel
source.release_notes: perf-canary now fetches per-model baselines and evaluates regressions with min-sample + rolling-window logic; emits structured regression verdict + attribution hints for dashboards/alerting.
source.description:
Implements the baseline lookup path and the first pass of regression evaluation logic for perf-canary. This is the missing piece between “we can generate load + emit raw latency/tps metrics” and “we can decide whether something is actually regressing (and do so with low noise)”. The evaluator is intentionally conservative for phase 1: it enforces minimum sample sizes, uses a rolling window over recent canary runs, and compares observed vs baseline using percent-change thresholds per metric.

Context / why: We’ve seen early canary runs spike due to batch composition variance and telemetry ingestion jitter. We need a consistent evaluation step that (a) won’t flap on small sample sizes, (b) is explicit about confidence, and (c) produces a stable verdict we can wire into alert rules + rollout gates.

What’s included:
- Baseline client: fetches baseline artifacts from the Baseline Store service (read-only) using a stable key.
- Baseline keying: model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hardware_sku (+ region when configured). The default is region-agnostic to avoid exploding baseline count; region-specific is opt-in per model via config.
- Regression evaluator:
  - Requires min_samples per metric (default 200 requests per window; configurable).
  - Rolling evaluation window: N most recent intervals (default 10m window with 1m buckets; configurable). Buckets are aggregated using trimmed mean for rate metrics and quantile merge for latency histograms.
  - Thresholds are percent-change relative to baseline, expressed per metric as {warn, page} (page is not used by this repo yet but is emitted in verdict payload for downstream alerting).
  - Anti-flap: uses “M-of-K” rule for verdict state transitions (default 3 of last 5 windows must violate threshold) and a cooldown period before clearing (default 15m) to avoid oscillation.
- Missing/invalid baseline handling: if baseline is absent, stale, or incompatible (e.g., schema mismatch), we emit a “NO_BASELINE” verdict and skip regression evaluation (still emits raw metrics). This is intended to be safe during early rollout.
- Output: emits a structured regression verdict (JSON log event + metrics) containing baseline stats, observed stats, computed deltas, and a confidence score. (Alert webhook payload formatting is handled in a follow-up PR.)

Metric coverage (phase 1):
- e2e_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99)
- runtime_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99) when runtime instrumentation is present
- tokens_per_sec
- queueing_time_ms (if present; treated as attribution signal rather than primary regression trigger)

Notes / non-goals:
- No baseline writing here (owned by baseline generator job / pipeline).
- No customer prompt shadowing. Canary inputs remain synthetic.
- Not attempting “auto-baseline drift correction” in this PR; we only validate staleness via baseline metadata + a max_age guard.

Config additions:
- baseline:
  - endpoint (required)
  - request_timeout_ms
  - max_age_hours (default 168)
  - include_region_in_key (default false; can be overridden per model)
- evaluator:
  - window_minutes, bucket_minutes
  - min_samples
  - violation_m_of_k, violation_k
  - clear_cooldown_minutes
  - thresholds: per-metric warn/page pct deltas

Testing:
- Unit tests for key construction + baseline deserialization.
- Golden tests for evaluator verdicts across common cases: below threshold, above threshold, too few samples, missing baseline, mixed metrics.
- Property-ish test for anti-flap (verdict only flips after M-of-K is satisfied).

Rollout plan:
- Run in observe-only mode for us-east for 2–3 models; validate that verdicts match expectation against staging baselines.
- After 1 week of stability, enable warning-level alert rules in observability-pack.

Screenshots / examples:
- Example verdict log line is included in docs/perf-canary/evaluator.md (shows per-metric deltas + confidence).

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4874 (baseline store + versioning), ENG-4873 (metric defs)
- Upcoming: pr-19244 (alert webhook payload), observability-pack alert rules PRs

Operational concerns:
- Baseline fetch is cached in-process with a TTL (default 10m) to avoid hammering the store.
- All emitted labels are cardinality-reviewed: model_id, model_version, runtime_build, hardware_sku, region (optional). No request IDs, no prompt hashes.

Requested reviewer focus:
- Nadia/Logan: baseline keying + staleness/compat semantics
- Sofia: rolling window choices + anti-flap logic (do we have enough dampening?)
- Allison: signal naming and whether the output shape is sufficient for downstream alerting/runbooks
commits: baseline: add BaselineStoreClient and key construction helpers
evaluator: add RollingWindowAggregator + ThresholdEvaluator with anti-flap
config: add baseline/evaluator config blocks + validation
tests: add golden tests for evaluator verdict cases
docs: add evaluator behavior + examples
conversation: Nadia Rahman (review): Baseline key looks right, but I’m worried about defaulting region out of the key for Dedicated pools. Can we at least support per-model override + emit the resolved key in the verdict? Also please add a max baseline age guard so we don’t compare against something ancient.

Noah Patel (author): Added include_region_in_key override per model (config) and verdict now includes baseline_key + baseline_created_at. Added max_age_hours (default 168) and evaluator returns NO_BASELINE if stale.

Sofia Ivanova (review): Rolling window aggregation: can we avoid simple mean on tokens/sec? We’ve seen spikes when a single bucket has low sample size. Suggest trimmed mean or weighting by samples per bucket. Also anti-flap: M-of-K is good; please ensure clear has a cooldown to prevent rapid clear/retrigger.

Noah Patel (author): Updated tokens/sec aggregation to trimmed mean (10% trim) and buckets are weighted by request count. Added clear_cooldown_minutes; state machine won’t clear until cooldown passes with non-violating windows.

Logan Wright (review): +1 on caching baseline reads; baseline store is not sized for per-loop fetches. Also please ensure we don’t emit region label when not included in the baseline key (cardinality).
Noah Patel (author): Baseline client now has in-process TTL cache (10m default). Region label emission is gated on config (only emitted when region dimension is enabled).

Allison Grant (review): For downstream alert rules, we need one canonical boolean-ish metric (e.g., regression_active{severity=warn|page}) plus a numeric “delta_pct” metric for drilldown. Can you add those? Also please link to ENG-4873 metric naming doc in evaluator.md.
Noah Patel (author): Added canary_regression_active gauge (0/1) with severity label and per-metric canary_regression_delta_pct gauges. Updated docs with naming reference and example queries.
CI (bot): buildkite/perf-canary-unit-tests failed: TestThresholdEvaluator_GoldenCases (schema mismatch).
Noah Patel (author): Fixed baseline schema test fixture (v2 field rename) and added backward-compatible decode for v1 -> v2. Re-ran CI.
CI (bot): buildkite/perf-canary-unit-tests passed; buildkite/perf-canary-lint passed; buildkite/perf-canary-integration-smoke passed.
Nadia Rahman (review): Looks good now. Approving.
Sofia Ivanova (review): Approved with the trimmed mean + cooldown changes. Please file a follow-up to evaluate whether 200 min samples is realistic for low-QPS regions.
Noah Patel (author): Follow-up noted; will add to ENG-4882 backlog once we have week-1 variance data.
Logan Wright (review): Approved.
Allison Grant (review): Approved.

- Regression evaluator:
  - Requires min_samples per metric (default 200 requests per window; configurable).
  - Rolling evaluation window: N most recent intervals (default 10m window with 1m buckets; configurable). Buckets are aggregated using trimmed mean for rate metrics and quantile merge for latency histograms.
  - Thresholds are percent-change relative to baseline, expressed per metric as {warn, page} (page is not used by this repo yet but is emitted in verdict payload for downstream alerting).
  - Anti-flap: uses “M-of-K” rule for verdict state transitions (default 3 of last 5 windows must violate threshold) and a cooldown period before clearing (default 15m) to avoid oscillation.
- Missing/invalid baseline handling: if baseline is absent, stale, or incompatible (e.g., schema mismatch), we emit a “NO_BASELINE” verdict and skip regression evaluation (still emits raw metrics). This is intended to be safe during early rollout.
- Output: emits a structured regression verdict (JSON log event + metrics) containing baseline stats, observed stats, computed deltas, and a confidence score. (Alert webhook payload formatting is handled in a follow-up PR.)

Metric coverage (phase 1):
- e2e_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99)
- runtime_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99) when runtime instrumentation is present
- tokens_per_sec
- queueing_time_ms (if present; treated as attribution signal rather than primary regression trigger)

Notes / non-goals:
- No baseline writing here (owned by baseline generator job / pipeline).
- No customer prompt shadowing. Canary inputs remain synthetic.
- Not attempting “auto-baseline drift correction” in this PR; we only validate staleness via baseline metadata + a max_age guard.

Config additions:
- baseline:
  - endpoint (required)
  - request_timeout_ms
  - max_age_hours (default 168)
  - include_region_in_key (default false; can be overridden per model)
- evaluator:
  - window_minutes, bucket_minutes
  - min_samples
  - violation_m_of_k, violation_k
  - clear_cooldown_minutes
  - thresholds: per-metric warn/page pct deltas

Testing:
- Unit tests for key construction + baseline deserialization.
- Golden tests for evaluator verdicts across common cases: below threshold, above threshold, too few samples, missing baseline, mixed metrics.
- Property-ish test for anti-flap (verdict only flips after M-of-K is satisfied).

Rollout plan:
- Run in observe-only mode for us-east for 2–3 models; validate that verdicts match expectation against staging baselines.
- After 1 week of stability, enable warning-level alert rules in observability-pack.

Screenshots / examples:
- Example verdict log line is included in docs/perf-canary/evaluator.md (shows per-metric deltas + confidence).

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4874 (baseline store + versioning), ENG-4873 (metric defs)
- Upcoming: pr-19244 (alert webhook payload), observability-pack alert rules PRs

Operational concerns:
- Baseline fetch is cached in-process with a TTL (default 10m) to avoid hammering the store.
- All emitted labels are cardinality-reviewed: model_id, model_version, runtime_build, hardware_sku, region (optional). No request IDs, no prompt hashes.

Requested reviewer focus:
- Nadia/Logan: baseline keying + staleness/compat semantics
- Sofia: rolling window choices + anti-flap logic (do we have enough dampening?)
- Allison: signal naming and whether the output shape is sufficient for downstream alerting/runbooks
text:
Implements the baseline lookup path and the first pass of regression evaluation logic for perf-canary. This is the missing piece between “we can generate load + emit raw latency/tps metrics” and “we can decide whether something is actually regressing (and do so with low noise)”. The evaluator is intentionally conservative for phase 1: it enforces minimum sample sizes, uses a rolling window over recent canary runs, and compares observed vs baseline using percent-change thresholds per metric.

Context / why: We’ve seen early canary runs spike due to batch composition variance and telemetry ingestion jitter. We need a consistent evaluation step that (a) won’t flap on small sample sizes, (b) is explicit about confidence, and (c) produces a stable verdict we can wire into alert rules + rollout gates.

What’s included:
- Baseline client: fetches baseline artifacts from the Baseline Store service (read-only) using a stable key.
- Baseline keying: model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hardware_sku (+ region when configured). The default is region-agnostic to avoid exploding baseline count; region-specific is opt-in per model via config.
- Regression evaluator:
  - Requires min_samples per metric (default 200 requests per window; configurable).
  - Rolling evaluation window: N most recent intervals (default 10m window with 1m buckets; configurable). Buckets are aggregated using trimmed mean for rate metrics and quantile merge for latency histograms.
  - Thresholds are percent-change relative to baseline, expressed per metric as {warn, page} (page is not used by this repo yet but is emitted in verdict payload for downstream alerting).
  - Anti-flap: uses “M-of-K” rule for verdict state transitions (default 3 of last 5 windows must violate threshold) and a cooldown period before clearing (default 15m) to avoid oscillation.
- Missing/invalid baseline handling: if baseline is absent, stale, or incompatible (e.g., schema mismatch), we emit a “NO_BASELINE” verdict and skip regression evaluation (still emits raw metrics). This is intended to be safe during early rollout.
- Output: emits a structured regression verdict (JSON log event + metrics) containing baseline stats, observed stats, computed deltas, and a confidence score. (Alert webhook payload formatting is handled in a follow-up PR.)

Metric coverage (phase 1):

- runtime_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99) when runtime instrumentation is present

source.title: Add mixed-length burst scenario + thresholds for KV compaction regressions
source.author: Rina Kobayashi
source.description:
Motivation: The KV cache compaction work (runtime-1.22) needs a perf-canary signal that reliably reproduces fragmentation + churn patterns we see in production, and that fails fast when compaction/eviction introduces decode stalls or tail latency spikes. Existing canary scenarios (steady decode, long prompts) don’t create enough allocator churn to surface the regressions we saw in staging.

This PR adds a new scenario (`mixed_length_burst`) that generates mixed prompt lengths + bursty arrivals + cancellations, and wires pass/fail thresholds + alert routing that are specific to compaction regressions.

What’s included: - New canary scenario: mixed-length burst (configurable length distribution + burst profile) - KPI collection updates: capture p95/p99 latency, TTFT, throughput, and the runtime-exposed compaction/eviction signals when present (bytes moved / compaction time / eviction rate) - Thresholds + gating: scenario-specific failure criteria (tail latency + compaction correlation guardrails) - Alert routing: send failures to `#eng-sre` + `#eng-runtime` with distinct annotation so oncall can quickly identify compaction-related failures vs baseline infra noise - Docs: scenario description + how to interpret failures + links to dashboards

Notes/constraints: - This PR does not require compaction to be enabled; it will still run against baseline runtime versions. When compaction metrics are absent, the scenario gates on latency/throughput only and emits a warning annotation rather than failing. - Thresholds were calibrated using recent benchmark-lab results and staging canary runs; see “Calibration” below.

Calibration summary: - Target workload: mixed prefill lengths (p10~64 tok, p50~256 tok, p90~1.5k tok), decode lengths with a long tail, 20–40% burst uplift for 90s windows, 5–10% cancellation rate. - Failure criteria (initial): (a) p99 decode latency regression > 12% vs control build OR absolute p99 exceeds scenario SLO cap; (b) compaction_bytes_moved_per_s sustained > budget + correlated p99 spike; (c) eviction_rate sustained above threshold AND output token rate drops below floor.

Rollout plan: - Land scenario behind `--enable-scenario=mixed_length_burst` flag for one week - Enable in nightly canary for Hosted GPU pools first; then expand to Dedicated canary cohorts once we have stable baselines

Related: - ENG-4828 (scenario) - runtime PRs: compaction metrics/tracing are landing separately in the monorepo; this PR only consumes exported telemetry when available.
commits: scenario: add mixed_length_burst generator + request schedule
thresholds: add scenario-specific regression gates + baseline comparison logic
alerts: route compaction-tagged failures to runtime+sre + add annotations
docs: scenario README + troubleshooting playbook snippet
ci: adjust runner timeouts for burst windows
source.conversation:
Marcus Reed (review): The scenario shape looks right, but I’m concerned the cancellation rate is too low to reproduce the relocation/cancel edge case we hit last week. Can we parameterize cancellation and add a short high-cancel burst in the middle?

Rina Kobayashi (author): Good call. Added `cancel_burst` phase (10s window) that spikes cancellation to 20% while keeping overall cancellations in the 5–10% band. Also added logging to make cancellation mix explicit in the run summary.

Sahana Iyer (review): CI runtime increased by ~9 minutes on my branch. Can we keep the default run under 12 minutes for PR CI? Also, any flake risk with the new burst phases?

Rina Kobayashi (author): Split the scenario into `mixed_length_burst_smoke` (PR CI) and full `mixed_length_burst` (nightly). PR CI runs a single 60s burst window; nightly runs 3 windows + longer warmup. Added runner jitter bounds to reduce flakiness.

Allison Grant (review): Please align metric naming in the annotations with the metrics standard (fragmentation ratio vs allocator fragmentation). If the metrics aren’t present, don’t imply compaction is disabled—older runtimes won’t export them.

Rina Kobayashi (author): Updated annotations: (1) uses `kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total` + `kv_compaction_time_ms_total` when present; (2) otherwise reports `compaction_metrics=not_exported` and links to the runtime version note. No mention of “disabled”.

Sean Gallagher (review): Alert routing: can we ensure these alerts don’t page the general perf-canary rotation if it’s clearly a runtime-compaction signature? Also we need the Slack message to include baseline build SHA + scenario seed.

Rina Kobayashi (author): Routing updated so compaction-tagged failures go to `#eng-runtime` + `#eng-sre` (no PD page) unless the failure is classified as infra (runner errors, timeouts). Slack template now includes baseline SHA, candidate SHA, scenario seed, and a link to the comparison artifact.

Marcus Reed (review): Thresholds: 12% p99 regression feels tight given normal variance on A10 pools. Can we start at 15% and tighten after a week of baseline?

Rina Kobayashi (author): Adjusted to 15% for Hosted A10 and 12% for H100. Added per-pool overrides in `thresholds/mixed_length_burst.json` and documented the plan to revisit after collecting 7 days of baselines.

Final: Marcus approved after changes; SRE/metrics approved after naming + routing tweaks.
source.release_notes: perf-canary: added mixed-length burst scenario and initial thresholds to catch KV compaction/eviction tail-latency regressions; updated Slack alert routing and annotations for faster triage by runtime + SRE.

source.title: perf-canary: add QPS rate limits + per-region capacity budget (GPU overhead guardrail)
source.author: Noah Patel
source.description:
Context: As we move perf-canary from staging into a production region, we need hard guardrails so the canary cannot consume meaningful GPU capacity or accidentally fan out due to config mistakes. This PR implements (1) explicit QPS caps with a token-bucket limiter, and (2) a per-region capacity budget that converts an overhead target (e.g., 0.5% of region GPU capacity) into an allowed request rate/concurrency for the canary. The goal is: safe by default, predictable overhead, and an immediate kill-switch when budgets are exceeded.

What this adds (high-level): - Rate limiting: token bucket limiter at the request scheduler layer (covers both steady-state QPS and burst). - Capacity budget: `capacity_budget` config per region (defaults to 0.003 = 0.3% until we tune) with optional overrides per tier/model group. - Budget accounting: estimates GPU-seconds/request using (a) last known baseline runtime-only latency for the model+SKU, falling back to (b) a conservative static estimate when baseline is missing. - Concurrency guardrail: hard cap on in-flight requests derived from budget; limiter blocks and emits a "budget exceeded" metric instead of pushing load. - Safety valves: (a) global max_qps, (b) global max_in_flight, (c) "safe_disable" config flag to stop generating traffic while still emitting health metrics. - Telemetry: new counters/histograms so SRE can confirm overhead stays under budget and catch misconfig quickly.

Operational intent: The canary should stay <0.5% of GPU capacity per region for covered models (goal in project doc). We are starting with tighter defaults (0.3% budget, max 0.6 rps per model group, max 4 in-flight) and will loosen only after we see stable overhead in us-east rollout.

Notes/assumptions: - We treat the canary as "worst case" for overhead: no reliance on customer KV cache, no warm batching assumptions. - If baseline is stale or missing, we err toward limiting harder (pessimistic runtime estimate). - This PR does not change the prompt distribution; it just changes how quickly we run it.

Follow-ups: - Integrate budget inputs with control-plane config surface once PR-19203 lands in `redwood` (config validation + audit log). - Add per-SKU budget overrides once we have eu-west A10 vs H100 variance characterized (ENG-4882).
commits: perf-canary: introduce RateLimiter interface + token bucket implementation
perf-canary: add capacity budget config + validation (defaults + hard caps)
perf-canary: budget-based concurrency limiter + block/skip behavior
metrics: emit canary_budget_exceeded_total and canary_inflight gauge
docs: update run instructions + tuning notes for us-east rollout
source.testing: Unit: go test ./... (incl. budget math + limiter behavior); Added deterministic tests for token bucket refill and for "pessimistic baseline" fallback. Manual: ran perf-canary locally against dev endpoint with `--max-qps=2 --capacity-budget=0.001` and confirmed limiter blocks with metrics incrementing; verified no spike in request volume during config reload.
source.rollout_plan: 1) Deploy to us-east canary namespace with defaults (0.3% capacity_budget, max_in_flight=4). 2) Watch dashboards: canary_request_rate, canary_budget_exceeded_total, and region GPU utilization (existing fleet panel). 3) If canary_budget_exceeded_total > 0 for sustained periods, lower budget or increase pessimistic estimate; do not increase QPS until stable for 48h. 4) Only after stable in us-east, replicate config to eu-west with separate overrides (do not assume SKU parity).
review_conversation: Sofia Ivanova (review): Budget math looks fine, but I'm worried about baseline fallback. If baseline lookup fails, we could still send too much traffic if the conservative estimate is too optimistic. Can we clamp to a minimum GPU-sec/request as well? Also please confirm this limiter sits before any per-model fanout loop.

Noah Patel (author): Good call. I added `min_gpu_sec_per_req` default (0.8s) and we take max(conservative_estimate, min_gpu_sec_per_req) when baseline missing/stale. Limiter is applied at the scheduler before target selection so we never exceed budget by fanning out.

Logan Wright (review): Label cardinality: please do NOT add model_version to budget exceeded metric. We already have model+region+sku in other places; keep this one coarse. Also can you ensure we can alert on "budget exceeded" without too many series?
Noah Patel (author): Agreed. `canary_budget_exceeded_total` labels are only {region, sku, tier}. Model-level detail is in debug logs + trace links, but metrics stay coarse. Updated accordingly.
Sean Gallagher (review): SRE concern: what happens if limiter is misconfigured to 0 and we stop generating traffic silently? We still need a clear signal that canary is effectively off.

Noah Patel (author): Added `canary_traffic_enabled` gauge (0/1) + startup log banner showing effective QPS/inflight/budget. Also `safe_disable` emits `canary_disabled_total` on each skipped cycle so it's visible.

Nadia Rahman (review): Please add config validation so capacity_budget cannot exceed 1% and max_qps cannot exceed a hard cap unless a feature flag is enabled. We’ve had "oops" incidents with canaries before.
Noah Patel (author): Added hard caps: capacity_budget <= 0.01 and max_qps <= 5 by default. To exceed, you must set `--allow-unsafe-canary-overrides=true` (explicit) and it logs WARN with config dump.
Sofia Ivanova (approval): Looks good with min_gpu_sec_per_req clamp. Approving.
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approving. Please make sure ENG-4880 rollout checklist references the new gauges.
source.release_notes: Perf-canary now enforces explicit QPS caps and a per-region GPU capacity budget, with metrics to confirm overhead and a safe disable switch.

source.title: Add synthetic canary for graceful degradation path (model/region fallback)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.description: Summary: This adds a new synthetic canary test that exercises Redwood’s graceful degradation behavior (fallback model and/or fallback region) and emits low-cardinality signals that map directly to incident taxonomy categories (Routing/Fallback misbehavior, Capacity/Fleet shortage, Control plane/routing config). Motivation: Recent incidents and taxonomy workshops highlighted that we don’t currently have a reliable, continuous signal that the “degrade gracefully” path is actually working end-to-end (router decision -> fallback selection -> request succeeds -> correct fallback_reason emitted). This canary is intentionally not a throughput/perf benchmark; it is a correctness + reliability smoke test with a small number of requests on a fixed cadence. What it does: (1) Sends a small chat completion request using a policy that prefers a primary model/region, but is allowed to fall back to a designated compatible model and a designated secondary region. (2) Validates that the response is successful and that the response metadata includes a structured fallback_reason when a fallback occurs (or explicitly indicates no fallback when not needed). (3) Publishes Prometheus metrics and structured logs that can be used in incident tooling/dashboards without exploding label cardinality. Key signals emitted: - perf_canary_degradation_success{tier="hosted",path="model"|"region"|"none"} - perf_canary_degradation_latency_ms_bucket{tier="hosted",path=...} - perf_canary_degradation_fallback_reason{tier="hosted",reason="capacity"|"health"|"policy"|"timeout"|"unknown"} (allowlist only; unknown is a single bucket) - perf_canary_degradation_expected_fallback_mismatch_total{tier="hosted",expected="model"|"region",observed=...} Mapping notes (incident taxonomy): - Sustained failures in this canary are an early warning for Routing/Fallback misbehavior vs Capacity shortage. The metric split (reason allowlist) is aligned with the router fallback_reason enums added in redwood, and is intended to support ENG-4824 taxonomy instrumentation. Rollout/ops: - Default: enabled in prod canary clusters at 1 req/min per region pair, small payload (<= 128 output tokens). - Alerting thresholds are intentionally conservative (multi-window, minimum burn) to avoid paging on transient blips; see follow-up in observability-pack. - Credentials: uses existing perf-canary service account and a dedicated routing policy ID restricted to canary traffic. Testing: - Unit tests for metadata parsing + allowlist mapping. - Integration test runs in CI against mock router endpoint. Notes: No customer-facing change. This is internal telemetry only.
commits: feat(canary): add degradation path synthetic test harness
feat(metrics): emit low-cardinality degradation metrics + structured logs
chore(config): add canary policy/region/model matrix + defaults
test: add unit tests for fallback_reason mapping and mismatch detection
docs: document dashboard queries + taxonomy mapping rationale
changed_files: internal/redwood/client.go (parse response metadata incl. fallback fields)
docs/metrics.md (add metric definitions and label constraints)
implementation_notes: The canary intentionally uses a fixed allowlist of fallback_reason values (capacity, health, policy, timeout, unknown). Any new enum values from redwood are mapped to unknown until explicitly approved (cardinality guardrail).
The test supports three expected paths: none (no fallback), model (fallback model in same region), region (fallback region). The policy config selects which path we expect per environment run.

Mismatch detection is tolerant of cases where no fallback occurs because primary is healthy; the mismatch counter only increments when the config explicitly forces a fallback scenario (via policy) and the observed metadata disagrees.

When fallback_reason is missing from response metadata, the test logs a structured event with observed fields to help debugging. This is treated as a soft failure in staging and a hard failure in prod (configurable).
review_conversation: Mina Haddad (Routing): Can we confirm the request actually hits the router path where fallback_reason is set? If this goes through any direct model pinning we’ll get false confidence.

Sergio Costa (Author): Good call. Updated config to use routing policy ID (no direct model pin). Added validation that response includes routing_decision_id and selected_model/selected_region fields; otherwise test fails with explicit error.
Allison Grant (Observability): Please do not emit raw model IDs or region pairs as labels. That will blow up cardinality. Keep path and reason only; anything else should be in logs.
Sergio Costa (Author): Done. Removed model/region labels; now only {tier, path} and {tier, reason}. Added structured log fields for selected_model and selected_region for debugging.
Aisha Bello (SLO/SLA): Add guidance for how this ties into incident taxonomy dashboards. Also, what’s the expected alert behavior?

Sergio Costa (Author): Added docs/runbooks/degradation-path-canary.md with suggested panels (success rate + mismatch + reason mix). Alerts are out of scope here, but I added recommended thresholds and noted we’ll wire them in observability-pack.
Rafael Mendes (SRE): This should map cleanly to ENG-4824/4828. Please add a short section explaining the taxonomy mapping and what ICs should do when it fires.
Sergio Costa (Author): Added “Taxonomy mapping + IC actions” section in the runbook, including quick decision points (capacity vs routing vs control plane) and links to the routing/fleet dashboards.
Mina Haddad (Routing): One more: ensure we don’t accidentally trigger large cross-region egress costs or load. Can you cap request rate and payload size explicitly?
Sergio Costa (Author): Added hard caps: max 1 req/min per configured pair, max_output_tokens=128, timeout=8s. Also added a CI guard to prevent increasing cadence without approval.
source.release_notes: Internal: Added degradation-path synthetic canary to validate model/region fallback correctness and emit taxonomy-aligned signals for dashboards/incident classification.

title: Add synthetic canary for graceful degradation path (model/region fallback)
preview: Summary: This adds a new synthetic canary test that exercises Redwood’s graceful degradation behavior (fallback model and/or fallback region) and emits low-cardinality signals that map directly to incident taxonomy categories (Routing/Fallback misbehavior, Capacity/Fleet shortage, Control plane/routing config). Motivation: Recent incidents and taxonomy workshops highlighted that we don’t currently have a reliable, continuous signal that the “degrade gracefully” path is actually working end-to-end (router decision -> fallback selection -> request succeeds -> correct fallback_reason emitted). This canary is intentionally not a throughput/perf benchmark; it is a correctness + reliability smoke test with a small number of requests on a fixed cadence. What it does: (1) Sends a small chat completion request using a policy that prefers a primary model/region, but is allowed to fall back to a designated compatible model and a designated secondary region. (2) Validates that the response is successful and that the response metadata includes a structured fallback_reason when a fallback occurs (or explicitly indicates no fallback when not needed). (3) Publishes Prometheus metrics and structured logs that can be used in incident tooling/dashboards without exploding label cardinality. Key signals emitted: - perf_canary_degradation_success{tier="hosted",path="model"|"region"|"none"} - perf_canary_degradation_latency_ms_bucket{tier="hosted",path=...} - perf_canary_degradation_fallback_reason{tier="hosted",reason="capacity"|"health"|"policy"|"timeout"|"unknown"} (allowlist only; unknown is a single bucket) - perf_canary_degradation_expected_fallback_mismatch_total{tier="hosted",expected="model"|"region",observed=...} Mapping notes (incident taxonomy): - Sustained failures in this canary are an early warning for Routing/Fallback misbehavior vs Capacity shortage. The metric split (reason allowlist) is aligned with the router fallback_reason enums added in redwood, and is intended to support ENG-4824 taxonomy instrumentation. Rollout/ops: - Default: enabled in prod canary clusters at 1 req/min per region pair, small payload (<= 128 output tokens). - Alerting thresholds are intentionally conservative (multi-window, minimum burn) to avoid paging on transient blips; see follow-up in observability-pack. - Credentials: uses existing perf-canary service account and a dedicated routing policy ID restricted to canary traffic. Testing: - Unit tests for metadata parsing + allowlist mapping. - Integration test runs in CI against mock router endpoint. Notes: No customer-facing change. This is internal telemetry only.

source.title: traffic burst shielding and graceful-degradation rollout guard
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description:
Motivation: we see a class of incidents where sudden client-driven traffic bursts (spikes in request ingress slope) overwhelm KV-cache memory and batching heuristics, producing high tail latency and queueing even when nominal throughput is within capacity. Existing canary detectors focus on gradual regressions or static threshold crossings; they tend to either miss very short sharp bursts or trigger hard rollbacks that are disruptive for live rollouts.

This PR introduces 'Burst Shielding' — a new detector + graded mitigation rollout-guard that: 1) detects sudden traffic bursts using a fused signal of p99 latency delta, request ingress slope, request queue depth, and per-device KV cache eviction rate; 2) classifies burst severity with a Nyquist-inspired adaptive smoothing and causal-change detector to avoid false positives on regular traffic ramps; 3) applies graded, time-bound mitigations that prefer graceful degradation over hard rollback: scale temporary concurrency tokens, disable batching for affected routes, route a percentage of traffic to a low-latency variant (quantized/FP16), and raise an alert with suggested manual actions.

What this change does (high level):
- Adds a new detector implementation: perf_canary/detectors/traffic_burst_detector.py that computes a fused burst score and severity banding. Uses EWMA slope estimation + causal change point test to detect sub-minute bursts.
- Adds an orchestration policy: perf_canary/policies/burst_shield_policy.py that defines mitigation steps per severity and a cooldown window.
- Adds config and rollout guard integration: deployments/canary/burst_shield.yaml (tunable knobs: slope_window, score_thresholds, mitigation_sequence).
- Adds telemetry: metrics/telemetry.md and exporters for burst_score, kv_eviction_rate, ingress_slope, burst_mitigation_state.
- Adds integration tests and an end-to-end harness: tests/integration/test_burst_shield.py that simulates traffic bursts at varying ramp rates.
- Adds runbook and docs: docs/runbooks/burst-shield.md and a short guide in docs/canary-patterns.md.
- Small runtime changes: a light-weight KV eviction metric hook in serving/runtime/kv_stats.c and a debounced batching toggle in serving/runtime/batching_controller.go.

Checklist: 
- [x] Detector implementation and unit tests
- [x] Policy orchestration and integration tests
- [x] CI flakiness investigation (initial failing e2e)
- [x] Docs and runbook written
- [x] Linear items linked

Safety and rationale for graded mitigation: instead of immediately rejecting the rollout, Burst Shielding prefers ephemeral, reversible actions that reduce tail latency without discarding a rollout (e.g., disabling batching for the affected route reduces queuing at the cost of per-token CPU, which is acceptable for short-lived bursts). Routing to a low-latency variant is limited by percentage and time to reduce correctness risk.

Backward compatibility: default policy is off for existing canaries; enabling is opt-in via the manifest. No public API surface is changed.

References: ENG-7842 (detection spec), PM-221 (customer incident summary), internal perf notes (link omitted).
commits: bfe9a2c: add initial traffic_burst_detector implementation and unit tests
c92d4b7: add burst_shield_policy and mitigation orchestration
d1a7f10: wire telemetry and exporters for burst metrics
e3f4c58: add integration harness and test cases for sub-minute bursts
f7a0b21: docs: runbook and canary-patterns guide
0a2bc9d: minor runtime hooks: kv eviction metric + batching toggle
9c4f001: fix e2e race causing CI flake (debounce window adjustment)
review_comments: Ethan Park (reviewer): Nice approach to fuse KV eviction into the score. Concern: how often do we compute slope? computing per-request might be expensive — consider a 1s aggregation bucket.
Maya Chen (author): Switched to a 1s aggregation bucket and added a micro-batching aggregation step in detector to avoid per-request overhead (see commit bfe9a2c).
Rita Solis (reviewer): Tests look good but I saw a CI flake on the first run — e2e harness timed out during the burst cooldown. Can we add a longer debounce for the CI runs only?
Maya Chen (author): Added a CI-specific debounce override in ci/e2e/profiles/burst_smoke.profile and adjusted the test to wait for mitigation state transitions (commit 9c4f001).

Jonah Meyer (reviewer): Suggest limiting the automatic routing to the low-latency variant to max 20% of traffic and 2 minutes per event. Also request telemetry annotation to mark which mitigation caused the change.
Maya Chen (author): Applied Jonah's limits to default policy and added mitigation_source tag on the exporter (burst_mitigation_state).
ci_checks: e2e: pass (flaky on first run, fixed)
source.release_notes: Added 'Burst Shielding' rollout guard: a detector and graded mitigation sequence for short, high-slope traffic bursts. Opt-in via canary manifest (deployments/canary/burst_shield.yaml). Includes telemetry (burst_score, ingress_slope, kv_eviction_rate), an integration test harness, and a runbook. Default behavior: disabled; enable per-canary.

source.title: Add region + hardware SKU dimension config (with fanout safeguards)
source.author: Noah Patel
source.release_notes: Perf-canary can now be configured to run per region and per hardware SKU with explicit fanout guardrails. Metrics and alert payloads include region/hardware context where enabled.
source.body:
## Summary This PR adds first-class configuration for running perf-canary across multiple **regions** and **hardware SKUs**, while ensuring we don’t accidentally fan out to every region/SKU combination (and burn the overhead budget). The canary runner now supports: (1) Target selection by `region` and `hardware_sku` (optional), (2) A hard fanout guard that defaults to conservative behavior, and (3) Safer defaults for Helm/Env-based deployments. This is needed for Phase 1 rollout where we want to start in `us-east` on a single SKU, then expand gradually to `eu-west` and additional SKUs once baselines/thresholds are tuned.

## Motivation / Context Today the canary targets are effectively “global” (implicitly whatever the control plane routes to). That makes it hard to: - Attribute regressions to fleet changes (SKU-specific) vs runtime changes - Set region-specific baselines (telemetry variance differs per region) - Control cost/overhead during rollout (avoid accidental wide fanout)

This change is intentionally scoped to **config + selection + safety rails**. Baselines/threshold wiring is handled by baseline fetch logic in a separate PR.

## Design - **Target dimensions:** `region`, `hardware_sku` are added as *optional* dimensions to the canary target config. If `hardware_sku` is omitted, the canary runs “per region” and relies on the inference tier routing for SKU; if provided, we force requests to that SKU via the internal routing hint header (same mechanism used by benchmark-lab).
- **Fanout safeguards:** - New config key: `fanout_max_targets` (default: `3`) - New config key: `fanout_allowlist` (optional explicit list of `<region>:<sku>` tuples). If set, only those tuples run. - New startup-time safety check: if computed target count exceeds `fanout_max_targets`, the process exits non-zero unless `--allow-fanout-over-max` is explicitly set (intended only for controlled manual runs). - New “blast radius” log line on startup prints the exact computed targets and the derived capacity budget per target.
- **Metric labels / cardinality:** Region is now a standard label on canary-emitted metrics. Hardware SKU is only emitted when explicitly configured (to avoid accidental cardinality growth); when omitted, we emit `hardware_sku="unknown"` for aggregator stability.
- **Alert payload:** Alert webhook payload includes `region` + `hardware_sku` in the `scope` section so incident-bot threads are dedupe-able per tuple.

## Config changes - New config schema fields (YAML): - `targets.regions` (list) - `targets.hardware_skus` (list, optional) - `targets.matrix_mode` (one of: `region_only`, `region_x_sku`) - `safety.fanout_max_targets` (int) - `safety.fanout_allowlist` (list of strings `region:sku` or `region:*`) - `safety.require_explicit_region` (bool, default true)

Example (Phase 1): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1"]   matrix_mode: "region_only" safety:   fanout_max_targets: 2   require_explicit_region: true ```

Example (Phase 2 w/ SKU): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"]   hardware_skus: ["h100-sxm", "a100-80gb"]   matrix_mode: "region_x_sku" safety:   fanout_allowlist: ["us-east-1:h100-sxm", "eu-west-1:h100-sxm"]   fanout_max_targets: 3 ```

## Implementation notes - Added `TargetMatrix` helper to compute the final run list. This keeps selection logic out of the request loop and makes it unit-testable. - Added request routing hints: `X-Redwood-Region` and `X-Redwood-Hardware-Sku` (only when configured). These are already supported server-side for internal callers; the perf-canary service account is restricted to internal endpoints. - Extended the runner loop to include `target_scope` in logs and metric tags.

## Tests - Unit tests for matrix expansion, allowlist filtering, max-target guard, and stable ordering (so rollouts are deterministic). - Updated config loader tests to validate schema errors (e.g., `region_x_sku` with missing `hardware_skus`). - Added a small “dry-run” mode test that prints computed targets and exits.

## Rollout plan - Merge behind conservative defaults: `require_explicit_region=true`, `fanout_max_targets=3`, `matrix_mode=region_only`. - Phase 1 (ENG-4880): enable `us-east-1` only, no SKU dimension. - Phase 2: introduce SKU dimension for `h100-sxm` only, validate baseline alignment, then expand.

## Risks / Mitigations - Risk: accidental fanout increases load/cost. Mitigation: hard guard + allowlist option + explicit override flag. - Risk: metrics cardinality blowup. Mitigation: hardware_sku label only when explicitly configured; region label is bounded and already present in most dashboards.

## Screenshots / Output (dry-run) - `--dry-run` output example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:*] target_count=1 fanout_max_targets=3` - `region_x_sku` example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:h100-sxm, eu-west-1:h100-sxm] target_count=2 fanout_allowlist=2`

## Follow-ups - Baseline store needs to key off (model, runtime_version, sku, region) once we enable SKU dimension broadly. - Confirm incident-bot dedupe keys include region+sku (separate PR in incident-bot repo).
commits: config: add region/hardware target schema + validation
runner: compute target matrix and include scope in logs
safety: implement max-target guard + allowlist filtering
metrics: add region label; conditional hardware_sku label
alerts: include region/hardware scope in webhook payload
tests: unit tests for matrix expansion and safety rails
docs: update README deployment examples for phased rollout
source.conversation: Vanessa Ortiz (review): Can we make sure there’s no way to deploy this to all regions by accident? The default config path is Helm values, and people will copy/paste. Noah Patel (author): Yep. Added `require_explicit_region=true` default. If regions are empty we fail fast with a clear error. Also added `fanout_max_targets` guard; default 3. Logan Wright (review): Region label is fine, but hardware SKU label worries me for cardinality. How are we controlling this? Noah Patel (author): SKU label is only emitted if `targets.hardware_skus` is set OR an explicit `matrix_mode=region_x_sku`. Otherwise we emit `hardware_sku="unknown"` to keep queries stable without blowing up. Nadia Rahman (review): For baselines, does the target selection order stay stable? We don’t want baseline fetch churn. Noah Patel (author): Yes—matrix expansion is stable-sorted, and allowlist filtering preserves order. Added tests to enforce it. Rafael Mendes (review): For on-call, can the alert payload include region+sku so incident-bot can dedupe correctly? Noah Patel (author): Added `scope.region` and `scope.hardware_sku` to the webhook payload. Incident-bot PR will pick it up, but this is backwards compatible. Vanessa Ortiz (requested changes): Please add a startup log line that prints computed targets + derived per-target QPS budget so we can sanity check rollouts. Noah Patel (author): Done in 8a2f4c1; log line prints `computed_targets`, `target_count`, `capacity_budget_qps_total`, `capacity_budget_qps_per_target`. Vanessa Ortiz (approval): Looks good. Thanks for the guardrails. Logan Wright (approval): OK with conditional SKU label + `unknown` fallback. Nadia Rahman (approval): Good to merge. Tests cover matrix/safety behavior.
