source.title: Rollout canary safety smoke tests (cohorting + auto-stop wiring) for staging
source.author: Leila Farouk
release_notes: Adds staging smoke tests that validate deterministic rollout cohort assignment and split accuracy.
Adds staging smoke tests that validate auto-stop/rollback signals are emitted and consumed by the rollout orchestrator without stalling.
Adds CI job wiring to run smoke tests on demand and nightly with automatic cleanup.
source.description:
Summary: This PR adds a small suite of staging-only safety smoke tests for Hosted canary rollouts. The goal is to catch two classes of beta issues before they reach customers: (1) cohorting / split enforcement regressions (e.g., observed split mismatch per region) and (2) stop-condition wiring regressions (e.g., SLO breach evaluates but orchestrator doesn’t pause/rollback, or rollout step stalls on metric timeouts). These tests are intentionally narrow, deterministic, and cleanup-safe so they can be run as part of GA change management.

Motivation / context: During beta we saw an incident-like split mismatch in us-east (SUP-48377) and a separate class of failures where metric evaluation timeouts could stall progress (see follow-ups in ENG-9158 / pr-28895). We already have E2E coverage in the monorepo (ENG-9164), but we wanted a lightweight “canary of the canary” that runs against staging infrastructure end-to-end (routing + telemetry aggregation + orchestrator actions) without needing full Console UI flows.

What’s included: 1) Cohorting correctness smoke: Creates a temporary Hosted route + rollout with baseline/canary config, sets split to 20% canary, and sends N=2,000 synthetic requests with a fixed cohorting key distribution. Validates: (a) per-request cohort assignment is stable for the same key across retries, (b) overall split converges to expected bounds (20% ± 3% at N=2,000), (c) split is consistent across regions when the same key is used (us-east-1 + eu-west-1), and (d) streaming requests retain cohort stickiness across reconnect within the allowed retry window.

2) Stop-condition wiring smoke: Starts a rollout step and intentionally injects failures into the canary cohort only (via the staging failure-injection header understood by the Hosted gateway) to exceed the default stop threshold for 5xx within a short window. Validates: (a) telemetry tags include rollout_id and cohort and are queryable via the staging metrics API, (b) stop-condition evaluator fires and emits an action event, (c) orchestrator transitions the rollout into PAUSED or ROLLED_BACK per policy (configurable per test), and (d) no orphaned rollouts remain after test completion.

3) Anti-cardinality + safety rails: Tests do not create unbounded (rollout_id, route, region) series. The suite uses a single route per run with a unique suffix, but aggregates assertions via the orchestrator + a single metrics query path. Added guardrails that hard-fail if route names exceed a maximum length or if run attempts to create more than 2 rollouts.

4) Cleanup and time-bounding: Every test uses a strict max runtime with deferred cleanup. If a run times out, we still attempt to rollback/delete the rollout and delete the staging route. Cleanup is idempotent and safe to re-run.

Implementation details: - Uses the perf-canary harness to call control-plane and orchestrator endpoints directly (no Console dependency). - Adds a small wrapper for deterministic key generation to validate hash cohorting behavior. - Adds a metrics polling helper with exponential backoff and a hard cap; avoids busy-waiting. - Uses a single staging-only feature flag gate so the job is a no-op in non-staging environments.

Changed areas (high level): - tests/rollouts/test_rollout_split_accuracy.ts: new split validation test and cohort stickiness assertions - tests/rollouts/test_rollout_stop_condition.ts: new stop-condition wiring test with failure injection - lib/staging/orchestrator_client.ts: helper for rollout create/start/pause/rollback + state polling - lib/staging/metrics_client.ts: helper for cohort-tagged metrics queries with bounded polling - lib/staging/cleanup.ts: idempotent cleanup for routes + rollouts - ci/nightly.yml and ci/on_demand.yml: new job definition + required secrets

Illustrative snippet (cohort stickiness assertion): The test tracks (cohort, request_id) for a stable key and asserts that retries do not flip cohorts unless the rollout config changed. This mirrors the ADR decision for deterministic cohorting and protects against accidental inclusion of unstable fields in the hash input.

How to run: - On demand (staging): ./scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh --suite rollouts --env staging - CI: job “staging-rollout-smoke” (nightly) and optional manual dispatch

Rollout / risk: Low risk to production since this is perf-canary only. Risk to staging is mitigated via strict cleanup and caps. If staging is degraded, the suite is expected to fail fast and page nobody (non-paging); it will file a CI failure for visibility.

Follow-ups: - Consider adding a latency regression stop-condition test once default thresholds are finalized (ENG-9158). - Consider adding a “split mismatch per AZ” probe once we have better visibility into the per-AZ routing logs.

Checklist: - [x] Tests are time-bounded and cleanup-safe - [x] Does not increase metrics cardinality unboundedly - [x] Validates both routing enforcement and telemetry stop-condition wiring - [x] Documented required staging secrets - [x] Linked to relevant Linear + support ticket
commits: Add staging orchestrator client helpers for rollout lifecycle actions
Add rollout split accuracy smoke test with deterministic key distribution
Add stop-condition wiring smoke test using failure injection header
Add bounded metrics polling helper for cohort-tagged queries
Add idempotent cleanup for staging routes/rollouts and cap run resources
Wire staging-rollout-smoke job into nightly + manual dispatch workflows
Tighten thresholds and flake mitigation (retries, backoff, timeouts)
review_conversation: Mina Haddad: The split bounds seem tight at N=2,000. Can we justify ±3% or make it configurable? Also please make sure the hash key input matches the data plane implementation (no request_id leakage).

Leila Farouk (author): Good call. I bumped N from 1,000 -> 2,000 and made the tolerance configurable per env with a staging default of ±3%. Also added an explicit assertion that the cohorting key is derived only from (tenant_id, route_id, stable_user_key) and not request_id. Added a comment linking back to the cohorting ADR.
Aisha Bello: For the stop-condition test: please ensure it doesn’t create paging alerts. Also we should validate the orchestrator transition, not just that metrics exceeded threshold.

Leila Farouk (author): Updated: the test uses the non-paging staging alert policy and only asserts via orchestrator API state transitions + action events. No pager integration is touched. Also added a guard to skip if the staging alerting backend is in maintenance mode.
Sean Gallagher: Cleanup needs to be bulletproof. What happens if the rollout is mid-step and rollback fails? We’ve had stuck rollouts in staging before and they’re annoying.

Leila Farouk (author): Added cleanup retry logic with jitter and a hard cap. If rollback fails after retries, we force-delete the rollout and route as a last resort (staging-only admin path). Also added a post-run sweeper that detects leftover resources with the test prefix and removes them.
Dylan Brooks: Can we align naming with Console/Platform? ‘pause’ vs ‘auto-stop’ semantics are easy to confuse.

Leila Farouk (author): Renamed the test cases to match product wording: ‘auto-stop (pause)’ and ‘auto-rollback’. Also updated the README and job output to print the final state using the orchestrator enums.
Logan Wright: Please be careful with metrics queries. Querying raw series per route+rollout can still be expensive. Can we query a pre-aggregated endpoint or enforce a single rollout_id filter?

Leila Farouk (author): Done. All metrics calls include rollout_id and cohort filter, and we use the staging cohort comparison endpoint rather than raw series. Added a hard fail if the query attempts to pull unfiltered route-level series.

source.title: Add predictive concurrency allocator with SLO-gating for canary routing
source.author: Priya Raman
source.description: Motivation: Canary evaluations often surface regressions caused by unexpected concurrency pressure on GPUs and model KV caches. This PR introduces a predictive concurrency allocator that uses short-window time-series features and a lightweight online estimator to predict occupancy and limit per-model concurrency before tail latency exceeds SLO thresholds. It ties into the existing canary pipeline so canaries can gate rollouts automatically when predicted saturation is likely. Design summary: - Runtime component: a new allocator service that subscribes to per-model throughput/latency streams and predicts short-term concurrency headroom using exponential smoothing and a small linear regressor. The allocator returns a recommended concurrency cap and a confidence score. - Canary integration: canary scheduler consults the allocator before opening traffic; if predicted tail-latency exceeds configured SLO margin the canary is paused and a soft rollback signal emitted. - Fallbacks: when the allocator is unavailable, we fall back to the static concurrency profiles. - Observability: new metrics (allocator_predicted_headroom, allocator_confidence, allocator_trigger_reason) and SLI dashboards added. - Config: exposed as a tunable in perf-canary Helm values with per-tenant overrides and a dry-run mode. Testing and validation: - Unit tests for estimator logic and boundary cases. - Integration tests in the perf-canary harness that replay historical traces and validate that the allocator reduces tail latency violations by ~35% in our samples. - Canary simulation suite to stress different traffic shapes (bursts, long tails, cold-start). Rollout plan: - Start in dry-run for 48h on canaries only (no gating). - Enable SLO gating for canaries with conservative thresholds for 2 weeks. - Gradually enable for low-risk models before wide rollout. Related work: builds on ENG-2134 (previous canary instrumentation) and complements the existing adaptive probe scheduler. Files changed (high level): list below details the main touched modules and rationale.
source.commits: 3 commits: 1) Add allocator prototype and API (perf-canary/service/predictive_allocator.py) 2) Integrate allocator into scheduler and canary gating (perf-canary/canaries/scheduler.go, redwood/runtime/scheduler/concurrency_manager.go) 3) Add metrics, helm values and tests (telemetry/allocator_metrics.prom, infra/helm/perf-canary-values.yaml, perf-canary/tests/test_allocator.py)
source.files_changed: perf-canary/service/predictive_allocator.py: new service implementing prediction and API; perf-canary/canaries/scheduler.go: consult allocator before opening canary traffic; redwood/runtime/scheduler/concurrency_manager.go: add optional cap honoring; perf-canary/canaries/allocator_canary.yaml: example canary config with slo_gating:true; telemetry/allocator_metrics.prom: new metric names and dashboards; infra/helm/perf-canary-values.yaml: new toggles and defaults; perf-canary/tests/test_allocator.py: unit and integration scaffolding; perf-canary/tests/fixtures/trace_replays/: new trace fixtures; docs/perf-canary/predictive_allocator.md: design doc and ops runbook
source.review_comments: Ava Chen (2025-04-11): 'Nice approach, can we avoid single-point-of-failure by adding local cache in scheduler?' -- Priya: 'Yes, scheduler keeps a 30s cached copy; clarified in PR.' || Diego Martinez (2025-04-12): 'Estimator seems to underpredict for sawtooth load; can we add a multiplicative safety factor?' -- Priya: 'Added safety_factor config (default 1.15) and tests.' || Liam O'Connor (2025-04-14): 'Please add more metrics for sampling rate and estimator uptime.' -- Priya: 'Added allocator_uptime and sampling_rate metrics, updated dashboards.' || Marta Iglesias (2025-04-15): 'Unit tests flaked once on CI, looks like a timing race.' -- Priya: 'Fixed test by introducing deterministic clock in test harness and re-ran CI.'
source.ci_summary: Initial CI: lint failed (style) -> fixed. Unit tests: some flakiness on estimator timing -> deterministic clock added. Integration tests: passed on second run. End-to-end perf-canary harness: passed with dry-run validation. Checks: lint (pass), unit-tests (pass), integration (pass), e2e-harness (pass), build (pass).
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash on 2025-04-18 by Ava Chen. Merge commit: 7f3a2d4. Notes: kept feature behind helm toggle and enabled dry-run by default.
source.release_notes: Enable predictive concurrency allocator behind flag. Adds SLO-gating for canaries that can automatically pause rollouts when predicted headroom is insufficient. Administrators should review new Helm values and dashboards before enabling for dedicated clusters.

source.title: Add canary for telemetry query latency (incident-bot diagnostics guardrail)
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.release_notes:
- Adds a perf canary that continuously measures telemetry query service latency for the incident-bot diagnostics workload.
- Emits p50/p95/p99 + error/timeout rates and opens an alert when SLO is violated.
- Provides a runbook and dashboard links for quick triage during incidents.
source.body:
## Context
Incident-bot v2 relies on the telemetry query service to render saturation/context cards in the first ~60–90s of an incident thread. We’ve seen query latency spikes during incidents (bursty usage + upstream timeouts) that cause empty/partial cards and slow responder workflows. This PR adds a dedicated perf canary to continuously validate telemetry query latency and error behavior under a representative diagnostic workload.

Related:
- INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts from incident-bot)
- ENG-5427 (telemetry query cache/coalescing)
- ENG-5429 (bot degraded-mode + load shedding)

## What this adds
1) New canary: `telemetry-query-latency`
- Runs every 60s (configurable) from 3 regions: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1
- Executes a fixed set of low-cardinality queries that mirror incident-bot cards:
  - GPU utilization (pool-level, region scoped)
  - Queue depth / scheduler backpressure (service + region)
  - KV cache pressure (cluster + region)
  - Error budget burn link generator preflight query (metadata only)
- Includes a small amount of concurrency (default 4) to catch p95 regressions without introducing significant load

2) Success criteria + SLO-style thresholds
- Records latency histograms for each query family + overall request
- Enforces guardrails:
  - p95 < 2.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - p99 < 5.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - timeout rate < 1% over 10m
  - non-2xx rate < 0.5% over 10m
- Canary marks a run as failed if any threshold is violated; emits a structured failure reason to make debugging actionable (e.g., `upstream_timeout`, `rate_limited`, `bad_gateway`, `query_budget_exceeded`).

3) Metrics + dashboard wiring
Emits the following (Prom-style) metrics:
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_latency_ms{region,query_family,tenant_scope}` (histogram)
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_success_total{region,query_family}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_failure_total{region,query_family,reason}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_payload_bytes{region,query_family}` (to detect accidental query expansion)

Adds a lightweight dashboard panel set (documented in the runbook) and a deep-link format compatible with incident-bot cards.

4) Alert
Adds an alert intended for SRE + telemetry oncall:
- Fires when p95 or p99 breaches for 10m OR when timeout rate breaches for 10m
- Includes annotations for top failing query_family and region
- Routes to `#eng-sre` and `#telemetry-oncall` (same routing as other perf canaries)

## Implementation details
- Canary uses the same auth mechanism as other perf canaries, but is scoped to a synthetic service identity with read-only access to the telemetry query endpoint. No tenant/customer identifiers are used.
- Query parameters are explicitly bounded (time window 5m, region scoped, no per-tenant grouping) to avoid creating cardinality pressure.
- Adds backoff + jitter when a run fails (prevents thundering herd if telemetry service is unhealthy).
- Adds an allowlist of query templates to prevent accidental addition of ad-hoc expensive queries.

## Changed areas
- `canaries/telemetry_query_latency_canary.ts`: new canary implementation + query families
- `canaries/index.ts`: registry wiring
- `config/canaries.yaml`: scheduling + regions + thresholds
- `alerts/telemetry_query_latency.rules.yaml`: alert definitions
- `runbooks/telemetry-query-latency-canary.md`: triage steps and escalation pointers
- `dashboards/telemetry_query_latency_canary.json`: panels for latency/errors by region and query family

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - threshold evaluation and failure reason mapping
  - query template rendering and hard bounds (time window, grouping)
  - retry/backoff behavior
- Local dry-run: `yarn canary telemetry-query-latency --once --region us-east-1`

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind `canary.telemetryQueryLatency.enabled` (default off)
- Enable in staging for 24h, then prod in us-east-1 only for 24h
- Expand to remaining regions after confirming alert noise and baseline distributions

## Notes
This canary is intentionally conservative: it aims to detect when incident-bot context gathering is at risk, not to load test the telemetry query service. If we want load-based validation, we should add a separate stress canary with an explicit budget and ownership.

---
### Commits
- canary: add telemetry query latency canary scaffold
- canary: implement bounded query families (gpu/queue/kv/error-budget)
- metrics: add histograms + failure reason labels
- alerts: add telemetry query latency SLO alert
- docs: add runbook + dashboard references

### CI
- build (node 20): pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- canary dry-run (staging): pass

### Merge
Squash merged into `main`.

---
### Review conversation highlights
Logan Wright: "Please double-check that the canary can’t accidentally introduce high-cardinality tags (tenant_id, model, route). We’ve been burned here." 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added a hard allowlist for group-by keys and a unit test that fails if `tenant_id|model|route` appear in the rendered query. Also capped time window to 5m."

Allison Grant: "Alert will be noisy if we page on single-region transient spikes. Can we require 2/3 regions OR a 10m window?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Updated rule to 10m and added annotations for top region/query_family; kept per-region firing but with a 10m window and higher p99 threshold to reduce flaps."

Miles Thornton: "Can we include a hint in the alert on what incident-bot symptom this maps to?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added alert annotation: 'Incident-bot diagnostics may degrade (empty/slow context cards). Consider enabling degraded-mode (links-only) if widespread.'"

Caleb Johnson: "Any risk that this canary masks real issues because it uses cache?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "We explicitly set `cache_bypass=false` to match incident-bot behavior; the goal is end-user perceived latency. Added metric for payload_bytes + noted in runbook how to check uncached latency if debugging."

title: Add canary for telemetry query latency (incident-bot diagnostics guardrail)
preview:
## Context
Incident-bot v2 relies on the telemetry query service to render saturation/context cards in the first ~60–90s of an incident thread. We’ve seen query latency spikes during incidents (bursty usage + upstream timeouts) that cause empty/partial cards and slow responder workflows. This PR adds a dedicated perf canary to continuously validate telemetry query latency and error behavior under a representative diagnostic workload.

Related:
- INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts from incident-bot)
- ENG-5427 (telemetry query cache/coalescing)
- ENG-5429 (bot degraded-mode + load shedding)

## What this adds
1) New canary: `telemetry-query-latency`
- Runs every 60s (configurable) from 3 regions: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1
- Executes a fixed set of low-cardinality queries that mirror incident-bot cards:
  - GPU utilization (pool-level, region scoped)
  - Queue depth / scheduler backpressure (service + region)
  - KV cache pressure (cluster + region)
  - Error budget burn link generator preflight query (metadata only)
- Includes a small amount of concurrency (default 4) to catch p95 regressions without introducing significant load

2) Success criteria + SLO-style thresholds
- Records latency histograms for each query family + overall request
- Enforces guardrails:
  - p95 < 2.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - p99 < 5.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - timeout rate < 1% over 10m
  - non-2xx rate < 0.5% over 10m
- Canary marks a run as failed if any threshold is violated; emits a structured failure reason to make debugging actionable (e.g., `upstream_timeout`, `rate_limited`, `bad_gateway`, `query_budget_exceeded`).

3) Metrics + dashboard wiring
Emits the following (Prom-style) metrics:
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_latency_ms{region,query_family,tenant_scope}` (histogram)
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_success_total{region,query_family}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_failure_total{region,query_family,reason}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_payload_bytes{region,query_family}` (to detect accidental query expansion)

Adds a lightweight dashboard panel set (documented in the runbook) and a deep-link format compatible with incident-bot cards.

4) Alert
Adds an alert intended for SRE + telemetry oncall:
- Fires when p95 or p99 breaches for 10m OR when timeout rate breaches for 10m
- Includes annotations for top failing query_family and region
- Routes to `#eng-sre` and `#telemetry-oncall` (same routing as other perf canaries)

## Implementation details
- Canary uses the same auth mechanism as other perf canaries, but is scoped to a synthetic service identity with read-only access to the telemetry query endpoint. No tenant/customer identifiers are used.
- Query parameters are explicitly bounded (time window 5m, region scoped, no per-tenant grouping) to avoid creating cardinality pressure.
- Adds backoff + jitter when a run fails (prevents thundering herd if telemetry service is unhealthy).
- Adds an allowlist of query templates to prevent accidental addition of ad-hoc expensive queries.

## Changed areas
- `canaries/telemetry_query_latency_canary.ts`: new canary implementation + query families
- `canaries/index.ts`: registry wiring
- `config/canaries.yaml`: scheduling + regions + thresholds
- `alerts/telemetry_query_latency.rules.yaml`: alert definitions
- `runbooks/telemetry-query-latency-canary.md`: triage steps and escalation pointers
- `dashboards/telemetry_query_latency_canary.json`: panels for latency/errors by region and query family

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - threshold evaluation and failure reason mapping
  - query template rendering and hard bounds (time window, grouping)
  - retry/backoff behavior
- Local dry-run: `yarn canary telemetry-query-latency --once --region us-east-1`

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind `canary.telemetryQueryLatency.enabled` (default off)
- Enable in staging for 24h, then prod in us-east-1 only for 24h
- Expand to remaining regions after confirming alert noise and baseline distributions

## Notes
This canary is intentionally conservative: it aims to detect when incident-bot context gathering is at risk, not to load test the telemetry query service. If we want load-based validation, we should add a separate stress canary with an explicit budget and ownership.

---
### Commits
- canary: add telemetry query latency canary scaffold
- canary: implement bounded query families (gpu/queue/kv/error-budget)
- metrics: add histograms + failure reason labels
- alerts: add telemetry query latency SLO alert
- docs: add runbook + dashboard references

### CI
- build (node 20): pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- canary dry-run (staging): pass

### Merge
Squash merged into `main`.

---
### Review conversation highlights
Logan Wright: "Please double-check that the canary can’t accidentally introduce high-cardinality tags (tenant_id, model, route). We’ve been burned here." 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added a hard allowlist for group-by keys and a unit test that fails if `tenant_id|model|route` appear in the rendered query. Also capped time window to 5m."

Allison Grant: "Alert will be noisy if we page on single-region transient spikes. Can we require 2/3 regions OR a 10m window?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Updated rule to 10m and added annotations for top region/query_family; kept per-region firing but with a 10m window and higher p99 threshold to reduce flaps."

Miles Thornton: "Can we include a hint in the alert on what incident-bot symptom this maps to?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added alert annotation: 'Incident-bot diagnostics may degrade (empty/slow context cards). Consider enabling degraded-mode (links-only) if widespread.'"

Caleb Johnson: "Any risk that this canary masks real issues because it uses cache?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "We explicitly set `cache_bypass=false` to match incident-bot behavior; the goal is end-user perceived latency. Added metric for payload_bytes + noted in runbook how to check uncached latency if debugging."

source.title: Add quantization profile canary scenarios (baseline vs profile-enabled) with thresholds + bisect hints
source.author: Jonas Weber
source.body: Summary: This PR adds perf-canary coverage for the Quantization Profile Library route-level enablement by introducing paired scenarios that run baseline (default precision) vs profile-enabled execution for the same model/workload mix, then compares deltas against explicit thresholds. The goal is to catch (a) tail-latency regressions, (b) decode throughput drops, (c) VRAM/caching regressions, and (d) new error modes (profile incompatibility causing unexpected fallback churn), before profiles are exposed to beta traffic. Motivation: With route-level quantization enablement landing across control plane + runtime, we need deterministic, continuously-running signals that quantify the risk of enabling a profile and that produce actionable failure output. These scenarios are intended to run on the same infra as existing decode/tps canaries and to surface “bisect hints” that point engineers to likely layers (policy resolution vs artifact fetch vs kernel path vs cache pressure). What’s included: (1) A new scenario type `quant_profile_compare` that executes two runs (baseline + enabled) using identical prompts and compares p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec, and error rate. (2) Scenario templates for two context regimes (short chat-style: 256 in / 256 out; long-context: 4k in / 512 out) to exercise KV cache behavior. (3) GPU matrix support (A100-80GB + H100-80GB) with per-SKU thresholds (H100 has stricter decode latency deltas due to kernel selection differences; A100 has stricter VRAM headroom thresholds). (4) Failure classification + automated bisect hints printed in the canary report, including common patterns: “metadata fetch spike / registry throttling”, “fallback rate elevated due to incompatibility”, “decode-only regression likely kernel path”, “prefill regression likely activation quantization / cache fragmentation”. (5) Wiring to export new comparison metrics so dashboards can slice by route/model/gpu and track trendlines over time. Notes/constraints: The scenarios default to running against a pinned model + pinned quantization_profile_ref in staging/perf-canary environments to avoid accidental drift. The scenario runner enforces determinism flags and pins seed where supported to reduce noise (still non-deterministic at token level; we only gate on perf/infra metrics). Thresholds were chosen based on last 14d baseline variance plus an additional buffer for multi-tenant noise; see `docs/quant_profile_compare.md` in this PR for rationale. Rollout: Add scenarios disabled-by-default behind `ENABLE_QUANT_PROFILE_CANARIES` until the control plane config endpoints are fully deployed in perf-canary. Once available, we’ll enable in staging for 48h, then production canary at low frequency before full schedule. Follow-ups: Add a third workload focused on tool-calling/structured output once the runtime path is fully aligned (out of scope here).
commits: a8c9d4e canary: add quant_profile_compare scenario runner
c1b2f37 canary: add baseline vs profile-enabled comparator + delta gating
9e4aa12 canary: add scenario configs for short + long context regimes
d02b1ee canary: export comparison metrics (quant_profile_enabled, delta_p95_ms, fallback_rate)
7bbff0a docs: add guidance on thresholds + interpreting bisect hints
1f6d0c8 chore: stabilize prompt fixtures + enforce seed pinning where supported
3c7fd21 fix: classify registry fetch timeouts separately from runtime 5xx
source.conversation: Connor O'Brien (review): Can we ensure these scenarios don’t accidentally run against an unpinned `recommended` alias? We should avoid silent drift in canary outputs. Also, what’s the plan if the control plane endpoint is unavailable in perf-canary? Jonas Weber (author): Good call. I updated the config schema to require an explicit `quantization_profile_ref` (no aliases) for this scenario type, and added a preflight check that skips (not fails) with a clear reason if `route-settings` endpoints return 404/501 in the target environment. Mateo Alvarez (review): The bisect hints are great but please make sure we’re not implying root cause too strongly. Can we phrase them as “likely” and include the key metrics that triggered the hint? Jonas Weber (author): Updated hint strings to “likely” and included the triggering signals in the report output: metadata_fetch_p95, fallback_rate, decode_p95_delta, vram_peak_delta. Hiro Tanaka (review): For decode regressions, it would help to print whether the enabled run actually applied the profile or fell back. Otherwise a large delta might just be fallback churn. Jonas Weber (author): Added `resolution_outcome` to the scenario summary (applied/fallback/disabled) and a per-iteration counter. Aisha Bello (review): Thresholds: please align error-rate gating with SRE standards (treat 429/503 separately from 5xx). Also add a hard cap on new 5xx spikes even if latency is within thresholds. Jonas Weber (author): Done: split error buckets and added `new_5xx_rate <= 0.05%` gate, plus a “registry throttling suspected” hint when 429 spikes coincide with metadata_fetch latency. Connor O'Brien (follow-up): Please add the flag to keep these disabled by default until we confirm prod scheduler capacity. Jonas Weber (author): Added `ENABLE_QUANT_PROFILE_CANARIES=false` default and documented enablement steps in the doc. Connor O'Brien: Approved after last push. Mateo Alvarez: Approved. Aisha Bello: Approved.
source.checks: CI: lint (pass); unit tests (pass); scenario dry-run (pass); typecheck (pass); docs build (pass). Initial run: scenario dry-run failed once due to missing env var for route settings base URL; fixed in 1f6d0c8 and re-ran successfully.
source.release_notes: perf-canary: Added quantization profile comparison scenarios (baseline vs profile-enabled) with explicit thresholds and actionable bisect hints. Scenarios are disabled by default behind ENABLE_QUANT_PROFILE_CANARIES.

source.title: Add cache telemetry overhead perf-canary gate (p95 regression threshold)
source.author: Marcus Reed
source.description:
Adds a new perf-canary workload and CI gate to quantify runtime overhead from the prefix/KV cache telemetry instrumentation. The canary runs a stable request mix with caching enabled and toggles telemetry flags to compare p95 end-to-end latency (prefill+decode) against a baseline. If the measured p95 regression exceeds the configured threshold, the canary fails to prevent rollouts that silently add latency overhead.

Motivation: We’re rolling out new cache metrics (hit/miss counters, eviction pressure, wasted prefill) behind flags and with sampling, but we need a hard stop in CI to keep instrumentation overhead near-zero (especially at p95/p99). This PR is intended to be the guardrail referenced by the runtime rollout plan and SRE approval thread.

What’s included: (1) New scenario `cache_telemetry_overhead` that replays a prompt set designed to exercise prefill, decode, prefix reuse, and steady-state KV reuse; includes prompt-length buckets (short/med/long) and a chat-like multi-turn loop to catch streaming-related paths. (2) A/B run harness: A = telemetry disabled, B = telemetry enabled at production defaults (sampling and rate limits on). Each arm is run N times and we take the median p95 to reduce flake. (3) Regression gate: fails if (B_p95 - A_p95) / A_p95 > 0.015 (1.5%) with a minimum absolute delta floor of 0.7ms to avoid noise-triggered failures on very fast hardware. (4) Artifacts: uploads JSON summary including p50/p95/p99, CPU util, and “telemetry event rate” counters emitted by the harness (not runtime metrics) so we can debug abnormal deltas. (5) Documentation: adds runbook-ish notes to the perf-canary README and exposes a single knob for temporarily widening the threshold via repo config (requires CODEOWNERS approval).

Notes/assumptions: This canary measures end-to-end request latency at the client boundary; it does not attempt to attribute time spent in metrics emission vs request processing. The goal is gating and early detection, not perfect profiling. The scenario uses deterministic seeds and fixed concurrency to minimize scheduler variance; it is pinned to a single GPU type in CI.

Rollout plan: Land in perf-canary first, run for 24h on main with threshold in “report-only” mode (configured via CI env var). Then flip to enforced mode once we confirm stability. This matches the broader cache telemetry rollout gate requirements (staging -> canary -> prod).
commits: Add cache telemetry overhead scenario skeleton and config plumbing
Implement A/B perf run harness and median-of-runs reducer
Add regression gate (relative + absolute floor) and CI wiring
Upload JSON summary artifacts + add README docs
Tighten scenario determinism (seed, fixed concurrency, warmup)
ci_checks: perf-canary (cache_telemetry_overhead): pass (enforced=false on this PR run)
source.review_conversation:
Allison Grant: Can we make sure this won’t page/flake due to normal diurnal noise? Also please add an absolute floor; on the smaller prompts the p95 deltas tend to bounce within ~0.5–1ms.

Marcus Reed (author): Added `min_abs_delta_ms` floor (0.7ms) in the gate and switched to median-of-3 runs per arm. Also added a warmup phase to stabilize caching behavior before measurement.

Omar El-Sayed: Threshold of 1.5% seems tight. What’s the expected overhead when telemetry is on with default sampling? Also ensure we’re toggling the same flags the runtime rollout uses.

Marcus Reed: Ran locally against a recent runtime build with the new cache metrics enabled at prod defaults (sampling 1/128 for per-request event logs, counters on). Observed ~0.3–0.8% p95 delta depending on prompt bucket. We’re toggling `RW_CACHE_TELEMETRY_ENABLED` and `RW_CACHE_TELEMETRY_SAMPLING` via the harness environment, matching `pr-18429` defaults. Added those env var names explicitly to the README so it stays in sync.

Sean Gallagher: I like report-only first. Can we enforce that widening the threshold requires SRE signoff?

Marcus Reed: Added `.github/CODEOWNERS` entry for `configs/regression_thresholds.yaml` requiring `@sre-oncall-leads` + `@runtime-owners` approvals.

Logan Wright: Please add a counter for “telemetry events emitted by harness” so we can detect cases where telemetry is accidentally not being enabled.

Marcus Reed: Added a harness-level event-rate metric (not high-cardinality) and included it in the JSON artifact + PR summary. Also added a failure hint in the gate output when event-rate is ~0.

CI note: First run failed due to a missing dependency in the runner image (pyyaml). Fixed in a follow-up commit; rerun passed.

Approvals: Omar approved after env var alignment; Allison approved after gate stability improvements; Sean approved after CODEOWNERS guardrails.
source.release_notes: Internal: Adds perf-canary gate for cache telemetry overhead. Fails CI if enabling cache telemetry regresses p95 latency beyond 1.5% (with noise floor).

(5) Documentation: adds runbook-ish notes to the perf-canary README and exposes a single knob for temporarily widening the threshold via repo config (requires CODEOWNERS approval).

Notes/assumptions: This canary measures end-to-end request latency at the client boundary; it does not attempt to attribute time spent in metrics emission vs request processing. The goal is gating and early detection, not perfect profiling. The scenario uses deterministic seeds and fixed concurrency to minimize scheduler variance; it is pinned to a single GPU type in CI.

Rollout plan: Land in perf-canary first, run for 24h on main with threshold in “report-only” mode (configured via CI env var). Then flip to enforced mode once we confirm stability. This matches the broader cache telemetry rollout gate requirements (staging -> canary -> prod).
text:
Adds a new perf-canary workload and CI gate to quantify runtime overhead from the prefix/KV cache telemetry instrumentation. The canary runs a stable request mix with caching enabled and toggles telemetry flags to compare p95 end-to-end latency (prefill+decode) against a baseline. If the measured p95 regression exceeds the configured threshold, the canary fails to prevent rollouts that silently add latency overhead.

Motivation: We’re rolling out new cache metrics (hit/miss counters, eviction pressure, wasted prefill) behind flags and with sampling, but we need a hard stop in CI to keep instrumentation overhead near-zero (especially at p95/p99). This PR is intended to be the guardrail referenced by the runtime rollout plan and SRE approval thread.

What’s included: (1) New scenario `cache_telemetry_overhead` that replays a prompt set designed to exercise prefill, decode, prefix reuse, and steady-state KV reuse; includes prompt-length buckets (short/med/long) and a chat-like multi-turn loop to catch streaming-related paths. (2) A/B run harness: A = telemetry disabled, B = telemetry enabled at production defaults (sampling and rate limits on). Each arm is run N times and we take the median p95 to reduce flake. (3) Regression gate: fails if (B_p95 - A_p95) / A_p95 > 0.015 (1.5%) with a minimum absolute delta floor of 0.7ms to avoid noise-triggered failures on very fast hardware. (4) Artifacts: uploads JSON summary including p50/p95/p99, CPU util, and “telemetry event rate” counters emitted by the harness (not runtime metrics) so we can debug abnormal deltas. (5) Documentation: adds runbook-ish notes to the perf-canary README and exposes a single knob for temporarily widening the threshold via repo config (requires CODEOWNERS approval).

Notes/assumptions: This canary measures end-to-end request latency at the client boundary; it does not attempt to attribute time spent in metrics emission vs request processing. The goal is gating and early detection, not perfect profiling. The scenario uses deterministic seeds and fixed concurrency to minimize scheduler variance; it is pinned to a single GPU type in CI.

Rollout plan: Land in perf-canary first, run for 24h on main with threshold in “report-only” mode (configured via CI env var). Then flip to enforced mode once we confirm stability. This matches the broader cache telemetry rollout gate requirements (staging -> canary -> prod).

source.title: Add canary check for output-token metering parity (streaming + non-streaming)
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.description:
Context: we’ve had at least one production regression where streaming responses under-counted output tokens in metering, which then cascaded into Unit Economics dashboards (blended $/1M output tokens) and reconciliation views. This PR adds an automated canary that detects output-token metering regressions before a gateway/runtime change is widely rolled out.

What this adds:
- New canary suite `metering_output_token_parity` that runs against a small allowlisted canary tenant in prod.
- Exercises BOTH streaming and non-streaming generation paths (including tool-call shaped outputs) and validates:
  1) output_tokens reported by the API response metadata
  2) output_tokens recorded by metering events (usage pipeline)
  3) that the delta between (1) and (2) is within a configured tolerance after accounting for ingestion latency
- Adds guardrails for ingestion lag: the canary polls the metering lookup API for up to 6 minutes (configurable) before declaring missing events.
- Emits a single structured result event per run (pass/fail + diagnostics) and wires alert routing:
  - warning to #eng-platform when parity drift > 0.5% for a single run
  - page oncall (telemetry) when drift > 1.0% for 3 consecutive runs OR when missing metering events exceed 2% of requests

Key implementation details:
- Request set: 18 requests per run (9 streaming, 9 non-streaming) with fixed prompt fixtures to keep variance low.
- Models: uses `rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct` (cheap + stable) pinned to a specific model version for determinism.
- Tokenization alignment: uses the server-side token counts only (we do NOT attempt client-side tokenization); the canary is specifically checking parity between two server-side sources of truth.
- Correlation: uses `x-request-id` and `metering_correlation_id` to look up metering records; falls back to time window + route + model + canary tenant ID when correlation ID missing.
- Flake reduction: retries on 429/503 with exponential backoff; run is considered inconclusive (non-paging) if infra is degraded and error rate > 30%.

Rollout:
- Enabled in dry-run mode for 24h (records metrics but does not page).
- After 24h, flip `CANARY_METERING_PARITY_PAGING_ENABLED=true` in the perf-canary prod deployment.

Notes / limitations:
- This is not a billing reconciliation test. It only checks output token counts parity between API-side accounting and metering events.
- Known source of noise: very rare duplicate metering events; the canary de-dupes by (correlation_id, sequence).

Operational links:
- Runbook draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/unit-econ-output-token-parity (will be finalized alongside ENG-8435)

Why now:
- The internal Unit Economics dashboard (v1) depends on reliable output token counts (especially streaming); this canary is intended to catch regressions like the one fixed in `redwood` PR-19355 before they impact exec/finance reporting.
commits: canary: add metering parity suite skeleton + fixtures
canary: implement streaming/non-streaming request runner and correlation ids
canary: add metering lookup polling w/ ingestion-lag tolerance
alerts: add parity drift thresholds + consecutive-run paging logic
docs: add README section + troubleshooting notes
source.review_conversation:
Allison Grant: Tolerance looks tight. Are we sure we won’t page due to metering lag?
Nadia Rahman (author): The canary waits up to 6 minutes for events and only pages on 3 consecutive runs >1% drift. Single-run drift posts to #eng-platform only. Also added an inconclusive mode when prod error rate is high.

Sean Gallagher: Please add an explicit guard for partial streaming truncation (client disconnect) so we don’t attribute it to metering.
Nadia Rahman (author): Good call. Added a fixture where we intentionally cancel a stream and assert that both API metadata + metering record the same truncated output_tokens; also tags those requests separately and excludes them from drift paging thresholds.

Logan Wright: Correlation fallback via time window scares me. Can we require correlation_id for the paging path?
Nadia Rahman (author): Updated: paging calculations only include requests with correlation_id present. Fallback lookup remains for debugging output in the log payload (non-paging).

Caleb Johnson: Add more structured diagnostics in the failure payload (model, route, streaming flag, expected vs observed tokens).
Nadia Rahman (author): Done. Failure includes per-request rows and summarizes top deltas; also includes metering record timestamps to see lag.

Allison Grant: Approved.
Sean Gallagher: Approved.
Logan Wright: Approved after correlation_id-only change.

Merged via squash.
source.release_notes: Internal: Adds production canary to detect output-token metering parity regressions (streaming/non-streaming) and alert Telemetry/SRE before broad rollout.

source.title: add-frequency-domain-residuals-detector-and-graceful-threshold-adapter
source.author: Miguel Torres
source.description: Motivation: transient, periodic interference (GC sweeps, noisy neighbors, periodic telemetry jobs) has caused low-amplitude latency regressions that percentile-only detectors occasionally miss and that can trigger noisy rollbacks. This PR introduces a new detector that analyzes latency time-series in the frequency domain and fuses spectral residual signals with existing percentile drift metrics to produce a more stable canary signal. Design summary: 1) Windowed FFT transform of per-request p95/p99 time series over short sliding windows (configurable); 2) Compute spectral residual energy in targeted bands (e.g., 0.5-5s cycles) and normalize by traffic power to be traffic-aware; 3) Produce a residuals_score time series and combine with percentile delta via a probabilistic fusion step (logistic weight based on signal SNR); 4) Graceful Threshold Adapter: thresholds are scaled by a lightweight traffic intensity estimator and by recent variance to avoid over-reacting to cold-start or low-sample windows; 5) Rollout guard: when residuals_score exceeds adaptive threshold for N consecutive windows we pause canary traffic and open an investigation alert with suggested probes. Implementation notes: detector implemented in perf-canary/src/detectors/freq_residuals.py using numpy/scipy primitives with a fallback optimized CFFI path for high-throughput hosts; fusion and threshold adapter live in perf-canary/src/eval/fusion.py; added PromQL metric exporters and Grafana panel JSON for visibility under observability/canaries/freq-residuals-panel.json. Tests and validation: unit tests cover spectral normalization, SNR-based weighting, and adapter scaling (perf-canary/tests/test_freq_residuals.py); integration canary harness added to perf-canary/integration/canary-sim that replays synthetic traffic with injected periodic noise to validate true/false-positive curves; load impact: CPU microbenchmarks show ~2% extra CPU on probe workers, latency overhead negligible. Rollout plan: feature flag behind perf.canary.freq_residuals.enabled; start with passive mode (score logged but not gating) for 48h in perf environment, promote to active gating on a small percentage of canaries, then global default after two weeks if false-positive rate < 1% and detection recall improves. Related work: builds on ENG-2480 (spectral baseline) and complements existing percentile-aware detectors in this codebase. Backwards compatibility: detector registers as optional evaluation plugin; default off for existing canaries. Metrics to watch: residuals_score, fused_canary_signal, false_positive_rate, mean_time-to-pause, CPU impact. Operational playbook: added runbook section pointing to runbooks/canaries/freq-residuals-runbook.md with RCA steps and suggested probes. This PR includes code, tests, panels, and runbook entries. Merge notes: squash-merged to main after approvals and green CI.
commits: Initial detector implementation and unit tests
Add probabilistic fusion and threshold adapter
Export PromQL metrics and Grafana panel
Integration harness: synthetic periodic-noise replay
Docs and runbook for operationalization
Fix: normalize by traffic power to reduce low-sample noise
review_comments: Ava Chen: Nice approach, could you explain why you chose the 0.5-5s band by default? Also please add a short note about sampling bias when traffic is bursty.
Miguel Torres: Chosen after empirical sweep on 50 canaries; added comment and default rationale to code and docs. Sampling bias mitigation added via traffic power normalization.
Priya Nair: The adaptive thresholding logic looks promising but we should be conservative for low-sample windows. Can we gate adapter aggressiveness behind a minimum sample count?
Miguel Torres: Added a minimum-samples parameter and a unit test; default min_samples=100 to preserve prior behavior.
Liam O'Connor: CI failed on linting for line length in the panel JSON; fixed in last commit. Approved pending CI.
Ava Chen: Approving — observed improved detection on the synthetic harness. Recommend a short training session for SREs on interpreting residuals_score.
Merged-by: Samira Patel (squash)
CI: All checks passed on re-run (unit, integration, lint, security-scanner)
source.release_notes: Add frequency-domain residuals detector and adaptive thresholding for canary rollouts. New metric 'residuals_score' and Grafana panel. Feature flagged disabled by default.
